gmw_00010949.name: RGZ J023832.6+023349
gmw_00010949.overview: RGZ J023832.6+023349 is a giant radio galaxy spanning nearly 2 Mpc in size [arXiv:1711.09611]. It is hosted by a quasar at a spectroscopic redshift of 0.209 and exhibits a hybrid radio morphology [arXiv:1711.09611].

sources.summary: The paper identifies RGZ J023832.6+023349 as a giant candidate hybrid-morphology radio galaxy with a FIRST core, NVSS-only diffuse southwest lobe, and QSO host at z=0.209.

quotes.quote: RGZ J023832.6+023349 (Figure  rys:hymors-large a): In the FIRST image the NE lobe displays a strong hotspot-like component at its far end. The SW lobe is not detected in the FIRST image, but faint diffuse emission is detected in the NVSS image with no compact components present. A compact radio core detected in the FIRST image is coincident with a mid-IR host (Table  tab:wise-radio ). This is a giant ( >1  Mpc) radio galaxy, with a size of almost 2 Mpc , and one of the examples where the low angular resolution NVSS images are needed for the classification of the radio source. This source is a QSO at a spectroscopic redshift 0.209 .

gmw_00010950.name: RGZ J072406.7+380348
gmw_00010950.overview: RGZ J072406.7+380348 is a quasar-hosted hybrid-morphology radio galaxy candidate located at a redshift of 0.241 [arXiv:1711.09611]. It features a compact radio core, a southwest lobe with a recessed hotspot, and a fragmented northeast lobe [arXiv:1711.09611].

sources.summary: The paper describes RGZ J072406.7+380348 as a QSO-hosted hybrid-morphology candidate with a recessed hotspot in its southwest lobe and fragmented northeast lobe components.

quotes.quote: RGZ J072406.7+380348 (Figure  rys:hymors a): The SW lobe features a strong component, which can be considered a recessed hotspot, embedded in diffuse emission that links the hotspot to the radio core. The NE lobe has no dominant compact hotspot-like sources and is broken into separate components in the FIRST image. A compact radio core is detected. The host has targeted SDSS spectroscopic observations; located at z=0.241 it is classified as a QSO.

gmw_00010951.name: RGZ J082231.1+531118
gmw_00010951.overview: RGZ J082231.1+531118 is a radio-galaxy candidate with a detected radio core [arXiv:1711.09611]. Its northwest lobe displays a terminal hotspot-like brightness peak, while its southwest lobe is plume-like and merges with the core at FIRST resolution [arXiv:1711.09611].

sources.summary: The paper presents RGZ J082231.1+531118 as a radio-galaxy candidate whose northwest lobe has a terminal hotspot-like peak and whose southwest lobe is plume-like.

quotes.quote: RGZ J082231.1+531118 (Figure  rys:hymors b): The NW lobe displays a brightness peak at its far end, which can be considered a hotspot-like component, with emission extending towards the radio core. The SW lobe is plume-like extending in the S and SE directions. A radio core is detected, but at the angular resolution of FIRST it is merged with the diffuse emission of the SW lobe.

gmw_00010952.name: RGZ J083352.2+045822
gmw_00010952.overview: RGZ J083352.2+045822 is interpreted as a single radio galaxy associated with a mid-infrared host [arXiv:1711.09611]. It lacks a detected radio core and features asymmetric lobes, though its structure could alternatively represent two separate radio galaxies [arXiv:1711.09611].

sources.summary: The paper treats RGZ J083352.2+045822 as a possible single radio galaxy with a WISE mid-infrared host, asymmetric lobes, and an alternative two-source interpretation.

quotes.quote: RGZ J083352.2+045822 (Figure  rys:hymors ): No radio core is detected, which makes the mid-IR host identification more difficult. In our interpretation the observed radio structure is that of a single radio galaxy with the mid-IR host WISE J083352.25+045822.7. The NE lobe hosts a strong hotspot-like component at its northernmost end. The SW lobe displays elongated, relaxed structure. We note that the structure could be also interpreted as coming from two separate radio galaxies, but in such a case the northern source would be composed of a single lobe with no distinguishable radio core.

gmw_00010953.name: RGZ J084738.0+183158
gmw_00010953.overview: RGZ J084738.0+183158 is a radio galaxy candidate with a radio core coincident with a faint mid-infrared host [arXiv:1711.09611]. Its northeast side features a strong, compact component, while the southwest lobe displays a relaxed, tail-like structure [arXiv:1711.09611].

sources.summary: The paper describes RGZ J084738.0+183158 as its weakest HyMoRS candidate, with a compact northeast component, relaxed southwest lobe, faint mid-infrared host, and possible optical counterpart.

quotes.quote: RGZ J084738.0+183158 (Figure  rys:hymors d): The side NE from the radio core is a strong, compact component, which could be considered a hotspot. There is an optical object ( with no mid-IR counterpart) in the vicinity that could be, in principle, associated with the NE component; however, the object seems to be detected in the SDSS images only, and it is classified within SDSS as a star. The SW lobe displays a tail-like, relaxed structure. A radio core is detected coincident with a faint mid-IR host. We found no optical or X-ray counterpart of the host in the publicly available surveys.

gmw_00010954.name: RGZ J085926.7+292738
gmw_00010954.overview: RGZ J085926.7+292738 is a radio-galaxy candidate [arXiv:1711.09611]. It features a compact radio core, a diffuse northern lobe, and a southern lobe containing a strong hotspot-like component [arXiv:1711.09611].

sources.summary: The paper reports RGZ J085926.7+292738 as a radio-galaxy candidate with a southern hotspot-like lobe, diffuse northern lobe, and compact core embedded in lobe emission.

quotes.quote: RGZ J085926.7+292738 (Figure  rys:hymors e): The southern lobe features a strong, hotspot-like component at its far end, with diffuse emission extending between the strong component and the radio core. The northern lobe is devoid of any strong compact component, displaying only diffuse emission similar to the lobe emission of the southern lobe. A compact radio core is detected, embedded in the diffuse lobe emission.

gmw_00010955.name: RGZ J091408.0+522948
gmw_00010955.overview: RGZ J091408.0+522948 is a radio-galaxy candidate that hosts a compact radio core [arXiv:1711.09611]. The galaxy features a hotspot-dominated eastern lobe and an elongated, plume-like western lobe [arXiv:1711.09611].

sources.summary: The paper identifies RGZ J091408.0+522948 as a radio-galaxy candidate with a hotspot-dominated eastern lobe, plume-like western lobe, and compact radio core.

quotes.quote: RGZ J091408.0+522948 (Figure  rys:hymors f): The eastern lobe is dominated by a hotspot-like component. The western lobe displays elongated plume-like emission with a surface brightness decreasing with distance from the core. A compact radio core is detected.

gmw_00010956.name: RGZ J094214.2+062751
gmw_00010956.overview: RGZ J094214.2+062751 is a radio galaxy with contrasting lobes [arXiv:1711.09611]. Its southwest lobe is confined and uniform, while its northeast lobe is elongated, diffuse, and relaxed with no easily distinguished core [arXiv:1711.09611].

sources.summary: The paper describes RGZ J094214.2+062751 as having contrasting lobes: a confined, uniform southwest lobe and an elongated, relaxed northeast lobe with no easily distinguished core.

quotes.quote: RGZ J094214.2+062751 (Figure  rys:hymors g): The emission of the SW lobe is uniform along its whole extent, displaying a confined structure. If a hotspot is present, it is merged with the lobe emission. The NE lobe consist s of an elongated, tail-like diffuse emission with the brightness peak in the inner part of the lobe, and shows a relaxed structure. A radio core is not easily distinguished and, if present, it is merged with the southern lobe of the source.

gmw_00010957.name: RGZ J103435.8+251817
gmw_00010957.overview: RGZ J103435.8+251817 is a high-excitation radio galaxy hosted by a luminous red galaxy at a redshift of 0.3941 [arXiv:1711.09611]. Its western radio lobe features a strong, hotspot-like component, while its diffuse eastern lobe is bent toward the south and merges with the detected radio core [arXiv:1711.09611].

sources.summary: The paper classifies RGZ J103435.8+251817 as a luminous-red-galaxy host and HERG whose western lobe has a terminal hotspot-like component while the eastern lobe is diffuse and bent.

quotes.quote: RGZ J103435.8+251817 (Figure  rys:hymors h): The western lobe has a strong, hotspot-like component at its far end, with diffuse lobe emission pointing towards the radio core. The eastern lobe is devoid of any strong, compact components, and its diffuse emission seems to bend toward the south. A radio core is detected, but is merged with the eastern lobe. The host has targeted SDSS Baryon Oscillation Spectroscopic Survey observations; located at z=0.3941 it is classified as a luminous red galaxy. This object is a high excitation radio galaxy (HERG; see sec:optical ).

gmw_00010958.name: RGZ J105521.2+372652
gmw_00010958.overview: RGZ J105521.2+372652 is a radio galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.5886 and classified as a quasar [arXiv:1711.09611]. Its structure features a compact radio core, a hotspot-dominated northern lobe, and a relaxed, diffuse southern lobe [arXiv:1711.09611].

sources.summary: The paper describes RGZ J105521.2+372652 as a QSO-hosted candidate with a hotspot-dominated northern lobe, relaxed diffuse southern lobe, and compact radio core.

quotes.quote: RGZ J105521.2+372652 (7C 1052+3742; Figure  rys:hymors i): The northern lobe is dominated by a hotspot-like component. The southern lobe consists of an elongated diffuse emission of a relaxed structure. A compact radio core is detected. The host has targeted SDSS spectroscopic observations; located at z=0.5886 it is classified as a QSO.

gmw_00010959.name: RGZ J120343.7+234304
gmw_00010959.overview: RGZ J120343.7+234304 is a low excitation radio galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.1767 [arXiv:1711.09611]. The galaxy features a faint radio core, a bright northwest hotspot-like lobe, and a bent, tail-like southeast lobe [arXiv:1711.09611].

sources.summary: The paper classifies RGZ J120343.7+234304 as a LERG candidate with a bright northwest hotspot-like lobe, bent southeast tail-like lobe, and faint radio core.

quotes.quote: RGZ J120343.7+234304 (Figure  rys:hymors j): The NW lobe is dominated by a bright, albeit extended at FIRST angular resolution of 5.4'' , hotspot-like component. In the NVSS image no radio emission is detected beyond that detected in FIRST. The SE lobe consists of a pointed tail-like emission that distorts and bends in the outer parts of the lobe. A faint radio core is detected. The host has targeted SDSS spectroscopic observations; located at z=0.1767 the galaxy is a low excitation radio galaxy (LERG; see sec:optical ).

gmw_00010960.name: RGZ J122425.8+020310
gmw_00010960.overview: RGZ J122425.8+020310 is a low-excitation radio galaxy located at a redshift of 0.4516 [arXiv:1711.09611]. It serves as the brightest cluster galaxy of the WHL J122425.8+020310 galaxy cluster and features an eastern lobe with a hotspot-like component alongside a straighter western lobe [arXiv:1711.09611].

sources.summary: The paper describes RGZ J122425.8+020310 as a LERG and cluster BCG, with an eastern hotspot-like lobe and a straighter, compact-component-free western lobe.

quotes.quote: RGZ J122425.8+020310 (Figure  rys:hymors k): The eastern lobe displays a strong hotspot-like component at its far end, with lobe emission pointing back towards the host galaxy. Some of the lobe emission close to the hotspot-like component extends sideways, to the north. The western lobe is devoid of any strong compact component, but it is rather straight becoming more extended sideways at the lobe far end. If a radio core is present, it is merged with the western lobe emission. The host has targeted SDSS BOSS spectroscopic observations; located at z=0.4516 the galaxy is a LERG (see sec:optical ).
The host of this radio galaxy is the brightest cluster galaxy (BCG) of a galaxy cluster WHL J122425.8+020310 .

gmw_00010961.name: RGZ J122653.9+041918
gmw_00010961.overview: RGZ J122653.9+041918 is a quasar located at a redshift of z=0.5174 [arXiv:1711.09611]. It is classified as a possible hybrid morphology radio galaxy featuring a southeast hotspot-like lobe and a bifurcated northwest lobe [arXiv:1711.09611].

sources.summary: The paper reports RGZ J122653.9+041918 as a rediscovered possible HyMoRS and QSO, with a southeast hotspot-like lobe and bifurcated northwest lobe.

quotes.quote: RGZ J122653.9+041918 (4C +04.43; Figure  rys:hymors l): The SE lobe is dominated by a hotspot like component. This component has been previously interpreted as a compact radio core, but no optical (SDSS) nor infrared ( WISE ) counterparts are found in its vicinity, and no radio variability has been found . The NW lobe displays a bifurcation extending north and north-west. We note this is a re-discovery, since the source has been marked previously by as a possible HyMoRS. If a radio core is present, it is merged with the diffuse lobe emission.
The host has targeted SDSS spectroscopic observations; located at z=0.5174 it is classified as a QSO.

gmw_00010962.name: RGZ J123300.2+060325
gmw_00010962.overview: RGZ J123300.2+060325 is a candidate hybrid morphology radio galaxy that lacks a detected radio core [arXiv:1711.09611]. This absence of a core suggests that the host galaxy's active galactic nucleus is currently operating at a very low level, if at all [arXiv:1711.09611].

sources.summary: The paper describes RGZ J123300.2+060325 as a no-core HyMoRS candidate and likely green bean host whose radio structure may trace AGN activity amplification.

quotes.quote: RGZ J123300.2+060325 (Figure  rys:hymors m): The NE lobe displays a hotspot-like component at its far end, with extended emission pointing back towards the host galaxy. The SW lobe is more relaxed, with no clear compact component. No radio core is detected.

RGZ J123300. 2 +06032 5 has no clear radio core detected down to the 3 upper limit of 0.5  mJy, what suggests that the activity of the host is at most at low levels, if present at all. Furthermore, the projected linear extent of the RGZ J123300. 2 +06032 5 radio structure of 255 kpc indicates that its radio jets were launched at least 4 10^5  yrs ago, wh ich might have coincided, or been directly related to, the high activity episode of the AGN immediately preceding the shut down. We suggest that RGZ J123300. 2 +06032 5 is a plausible example of the formation of HyMoRS through the amplification of the AGN activity, which we propose as an additional possible mechanism in this paper.

gmw_00010963.name: RGZ J123414.7+222248
gmw_00010963.overview: RGZ J123414.7+222248 is a radio-galaxy candidate [arXiv:1711.09611]. The galaxy features a compact radio core, a strong northern hotspot-like lobe, and a southern lobe characterized by diffuse meandering emission [arXiv:1711.09611].

sources.summary: The paper identifies RGZ J123414.7+222248 as a radio-galaxy candidate with a strong northern hotspot-like lobe, diffuse meandering southern lobe, and compact core.

quotes.quote: RGZ J123414.7+222248 (7C 1231+2239; Figure  rys:hymors n): The northern lobe is dominated by a strong hotspot-like component. The southern lobe displays diffuse meandering emission. A compact radio core is detected.

gmw_00010964.name: RGZ J131414.1+020404
gmw_00010964.overview: RGZ J131414.1+020404 is a radio-galaxy candidate that hosts a compact radio core [arXiv:1711.09611]. Its structure features a southeast lobe with a strong hotspot-like component and a bent, tail-like northwest lobe [arXiv:1711.09611].

sources.summary: The paper presents RGZ J131414.1+020404 as a radio-galaxy candidate with a southeast hotspot-like lobe, bent tail-like northwest lobe, and compact radio core.

quotes.quote: RGZ J131414.1+020404 (Figure  rys:hymors o): The SE lobe displays a strong hotspot-like component, with low-level emission pointing towards the radio core. The NW lobe is bent and is of a tail-like structure. A compact radio core is detected.

gmw_00010965.name: RGZ J144300.1+144042
gmw_00010965.overview: RGZ J144300.1+144042 is a radio galaxy candidate that resembles a hybrid morphology radio source [arXiv:1711.09611]. The galaxy lacks a detected radio core, featuring a resolved northern lobe component and an elongated, diffuse southern lobe [arXiv:1711.09611].

sources.summary: The paper describes RGZ J144300.1+144042 as a no-core HyMoRS-like candidate, with a resolved northern component and elongated diffuse southern lobe.

quotes.quote: RGZ J144300.1+144042 (Figure  rys:hymors p): The northern lobe displays a bright component, which is resolved at the FIRST angular resolution of 5.4'' . No additional emission is detected in NVSS beyond the FIRST component. The southern lobe displays elongated diffuse emission. No radio core is detected. This source is, to some degree, reminiscent of the HyMoRS reported by .

gmw_00010966.name: RGZ J144921.5+501945
gmw_00010966.overview: RGZ J144921.5+501945 is a radio galaxy that has been classified as a possible BL Lac object [arXiv:1711.09611]. It features a compact radio core, a diffuse southwest lobe, and a northeast lobe containing a hotspot-like component [arXiv:1711.09611].

sources.summary: The paper reports RGZ J144921.5+501945 as a possible BL Lac radio galaxy with a hotspot-like northeast lobe, diffuse southwest lobe, and compact core.

quotes.quote: RGZ J144921.5+501945 (Figure  rys:hymors q): The NE lobe displays a strong hotspot-like component at its far end, with lobe diffuse emission extending both towards the radio core and sideways , perhaps representing a backflow . The SW lobe has no hotspot-like components, and its brightest peak is located close to the radio core. A compact radio core is detected. This radio galaxy has been previo us ly classified as a possible BL Lac object .

gmw_00010967.name: RGZ J150407.5+574918
gmw_00010967.overview: RGZ J150407.5+574918 is a radio galaxy candidate with an uncertain radio core [arXiv:1711.09611]. Its structure features a northeast lobe with a hotspot-like component and a relaxed, plume-like southwest lobe [arXiv:1711.09611].

sources.summary: The paper identifies RGZ J150407.5+574918 as a radio-galaxy candidate with a northeast hotspot-like lobe, relaxed plume-like southwest lobe, uncertain core, and confused mid-infrared counterpart.

quotes.quote: RGZ J150407.5+574918 (Figure  rys:hymors ): The NE lobe displays a hotspot-like component at its far end, and more diffuse lobe emission pointing towards the position of the host galaxy, but also extending somewhat sideways. The SW lobe is of a plume-like, relaxed and meandering structure. No clear radio core is detected, but the host is most likely SDSSJ150408.08+574922.5. The mid-IR counterpart is confused with a nearby galaxy.

gmw_00010968.name: RGZ J150455.5+564920
gmw_00010968.overview: RGZ J150455.5+564920 is a broadline quasar located at a redshift of 0.3587 [arXiv:1711.09611]. Its radio structure features a bent, hotspot-like southeastern lobe and a more diffuse, relaxed northwestern lobe [arXiv:1711.09611].

sources.summary: The paper describes RGZ J150455.5+564920 as a broadline QSO candidate with a bent southeast hotspot-like lobe and more diffuse relaxed northwest lobe.

quotes.quote: RGZ J150455.5+564920 (Figure  rys:hymors s): The SE lobe displays a strong hotspot-like component, with extended emission bent and pointing towards the direction of the host galaxy. The NW lobe is of more extended, diffuse and relaxed structure, with brightness peak occuring close to the position of the host galaxy. If a radio core is present, it is merged with the NW lobe emission. The host has targeted SDSS spectroscopic observations; located at z=0.3587 it is classified as a broadline QSO.

gmw_00010969.name: RGZ J151136.9+335501
gmw_00010969.overview: RGZ J151136.9+335501 is a radio galaxy hosted by a quasar at a redshift of 0.6234 [arXiv:1711.09611]. Its southwest radio lobe features a hotspot-like component, while its northeast lobe exhibits a plume-like structure [arXiv:1711.09611].

sources.summary: The paper reports RGZ J151136.9+335501 as a QSO-hosted candidate whose southwest lobe has a hotspot-like component and whose northeast lobe is plume-like.

quotes.quote: RGZ J151136.9+335501 (7C 1509+3406; Figure  rys:hymors t): The SW lobe displays a hotspot-like component with diffuse lobe emission pointing eastwards. The NE lobe is of a plume-like structure. A radio core is not easily distinguishable, and if present it is merged with the diffuse emission connecting the lobes of the radio galaxy. The host has targeted SDSS spectroscopic observations; located at z=0.6234 it is classified as a QSO.

gmw_00010970.name: RGZ J152737.1+182250
gmw_00010970.overview: RGZ J152737.1+182250 is a radio galaxy candidate that hosts a strong, compact radio core [arXiv:1711.09611]. Its northeast lobe features a hotspot-like component with extended, bending emission, while the southwest lobe displays a brightness peak near the core [arXiv:1711.09611].

sources.summary: The paper identifies RGZ J152737.1+182250 as a radio-galaxy candidate with a bent northeast hotspot-like lobe, southwest brightness peak near the core, and strong compact core.

quotes.quote: RGZ J152737.1+182250 (Figure  rys:hymors u): The NE lobe displays a hotspot-like component at its far end, with extended emission bending and extending south, but slightly pointing towards the radio core. The SW lobe features a brightness peak in the proximity of the srong radio core, with some detached emission further away. A strong, compact radio core is detected.

gmw_00010971.name: RGZ J153421.4+333436
gmw_00010971.overview: RGZ J153421.4+333436 is a radio-galaxy candidate that hosts a detected radio core [arXiv:1711.09611]. The galaxy features an eastern lobe with a terminal hotspot and a western lobe with diffuse emission extending far beyond its brightness peak [arXiv:1711.09611].

sources.summary: The paper describes RGZ J153421.4+333436 as a radio-galaxy candidate with an eastern terminal hotspot, extended western lobe emission, and a core embedded in diffuse emission.

quotes.quote: RGZ J153421.4+333436 (7C 1532+3344; Figure  rys:hymors v): The eastern lobe features a strong hotspot-like component at its far end, with diffuse lobe emission extending between the hotspot and the radio core. The western lobe features a brightness peak close to the radio core, and displays diffuse emission extending far beyond the brightness peak, away f ro m the radio core. A radio core is detected, but is embedded in the diffuse lobe emission.

gmw_00010972.name: RGZ J170002.6+270549
gmw_00010972.overview: RGZ J170002.6+270549 is a radio-galaxy candidate with a detected radio core [arXiv:1711.09611]. Its northeast lobe features an extended strong component, while its southwest lobe consists of a tail-like structure meandering away from the core [arXiv:1711.09611].

sources.summary: The paper presents RGZ J170002.6+270549 as a radio-galaxy candidate with an extended strong northeast component, tail-like meandering southwest lobe, and detected radio core.

quotes.quote: RGZ J170002.6+270549 (Figure  rys:hymors w): The NE lobe features a single, but somewhat extended strong component. The SW lobe is devoid of compact components and consists of a tail-like structure slightly meandering away from the radio core. A radio core is detected.

gmw_00010973.name: z=0.91 low-mass star-forming galaxy
aliases_external: obj027 / z=0.909 OII host galaxy toward SDSS J142253.31-000149
gmw_00010973.overview: This z=0.91 low-mass star-forming galaxy is isolated, with no companion galaxies detected within 240 kiloparsecs [arXiv:1601.07567]. It exhibits a dust-corrected star formation rate of 4.7 solar masses per year and features a wind with an estimated mass outflow rate of 0.5 to 5 solar masses per year [arXiv:1601.07567].

sources.summary: The paper studies a z=0.91 star-forming galaxy with MUSE and quasar absorption, finding isolation, modest extinction, solar metallicity, rotation, outflow, and co-rotating circumgalactic gas.

quotes.quote: We use a background quasar to detect the presence of circumgalactic gas around a z=0.91 low-mass star-forming galaxy. Data from the new Multi Unit Spectroscopic Explorer (MUSE) on the Very Large Telescope show that the galaxy has a dust-corrected star formation rate (SFR) of , with no companion down to 0.22 \ (5 ) within 240 \ (30 ). Using a high-resolution spectrum (UVES) of the background quasar, which is fortuitously aligned with the galaxy major axis (with an azimuth angle of only 15^ ), we find, in the gas kinematics traced by low-ionization lines, distinct signatures consistent with those expected for a ``cold-flow disk'' extending at least 12 kpc ( 3 R_ 1/2 ).

In summary, thanks to our new VLT/MUSE data and our ancillary SINFONI data, we found that the z_ sys = host galaxy of this galaxy--quasar pair is isolated with no neighbors within 240 kpc down to a SFR of 0.22 \ ( 5 ), as shown in Figure fig:musefield:narrow ; has a dust-corrected SFR of , using a ChabrierG_03a IMF, and a small amount of extinction E(B-V)=0.1 0.1 mag; has a solar metallicity ( 12+ O/H =8.7 0.2 ) from an analysis of the nebular emission lines detected in MUSE ( , ) and SINFONI ( ), as shown in Figure fig:metallicity ; has a maximum rotation velocity of V_ max 100 10 , corresponding to a halo mass of M_ h 2 10^ 11 \ or to a 0.1 L_ galaxy, and an inclination of about i 60^ simultaneously determined through 3D modeling (Figure fig:maps ); has a wind with an estimated mass outflow rate of 0.5--5 \ (i.e. a loading factor 1 ) from the blueshifted (by v_ out =-80 15 ) low-ionization absorptions ( \ and ) in the MUSE galaxy spectrum (Figure fig:muse:spectra ).

The analysis of the quasar absorption profile obtained with the VLT/UVES spectrograph shows distinct signatures of co-planar gas that appears to be co-rotating with the host galaxy, but at a speed lower than the rotation velocity (Figure fig:mgi a) from the low-ionization metal absorption lines; that the metallicity of the absorbing gas is estimated at about =-0.4 0.4 (0.4 Z_ ; Figures fig:uves and fig:jenkins ) globally across the profile We are able to constrain the relative metallicity across the absorption profile (Figure fig:mgi b), but not the absolute metallicity.

gmw_00010974.name: par0750+2917 1736
gmw_00010974.overview: The candidate galaxy par0750+2917 1736 has a best-fit redshift of 8.40 [arXiv:2002.06209]. Although previously suspected of flux variability, its resolved size and photometry support a high-redshift nature rather than a flaring brown dwarf [arXiv:2002.06209].

sources.summary: The paper reports par0750+2917 1736 as a z_best 8.40 candidate; despite earlier variability concerns, its resolved size and BoRG/IRAC photometry support a high-redshift solution.

quotes.quote: Candidate par0750+2917 1736 has mag _ H_ 160 = 26.2 and z_ best = 8.40. This candidate had been previously observed in HIPPIES programs 11700 and 11702, but was discarded as a possible high redshift galaxy because it showed flux variability in the H_ 160 filter. BoRG [ z910 ] has data from only one epoch for this candidate and therefore we cannot probe variability. However, our analysis indicates that this candidate appears to be resolved as seen in Figure star_yj , making it unlikely for this object to be a flaring brown dwarf, which was the explanation by Yan2011 . Additionally, we note that HIPPIES did not have the JH_ 140 filter or IRAC 3.6 which in this BoRG work supports the high redshift solution for this candidate.

gmw_00010975.name: par0953+5153 1655
gmw_00010975.overview: The high-redshift galaxy candidate par0953+5153 1655 has a best-fit photometric redshift of 7.81 [arXiv:2002.06209]. It is likely magnified by gravitational lensing from a massive neighbor, which also introduces flux contamination to its photometric measurements [arXiv:2002.06209].

sources.summary: The paper keeps par0953+5153 1655 as a z_best 7.81 candidate, noting ambiguous IRAC flux assignment, possible lensing magnification, and likely neighboring contamination in one fit.

quotes.quote: The best-fit Galfit model for one observation of candidate par0953+5153 1655 attributes all IRAC flux to a bright neighbor (top right), while the best-fit model for the second observation (bottom right) associates some flux with the candidate. While we include both flux values in our updated photometric redshift fit, we note that the flux attributed to the candidate is not centered at the expected position of the candidate, implying that the flux in fact belongs to the neighbor.

Candidate par0953+5153 1655 has z_ best = 7.81 and mag _ H_ 160 = 25.8, while candidate par0956+2847 169 has z_ best = 7.78 with mag _ H_ 160 = 25.3. Both of these sources are also likely magnified by a massive neighbor, we discuss these magnification corrections in Section magnification .

gmw_00010976.name: J160810+352809
gmw_00010976.overview: J160810+352809 is a highly ionized Green Pea dwarf galaxy experiencing a brief period of intense star formation [arXiv:1902.08204]. It has an anomalously high specific star-formation rate for its neutral hydrogen mass, which is constrained to an upper limit of 10 to the power of 8.14 solar masses [arXiv:1902.08204].

sources.summary: The paper presents VLA observations of J160810+352809, finding extreme ionization, an HI mass upper limit, unusually high specific star formation for its HI mass, and possible conditions favoring LyC escape.

quotes.quote: To study these processes we analyze VLA observations of one Green Pea (GP), J160810+352809 (hereafter J1608), and HST COS spectra of 17 GP galaxies at z<0.2 . All are highly ionized: J1608 has the highest [ O III ] 5007 / [ O II ] 3727 for star-forming galaxies in SDSS, and the 17 GPs have 6.6 . We set an upper limit on J1608's HI mass of M_ HI /M_ =8.4 , near or below average compared to similar mass dwarf galaxies.

We do not detect emission in J1608, and place a 3 upper limit on the HI mass of M_ HI /M_ <8.14 . This limit is consistent with the HI content of blue compact dwarfs and HI-selected dwarf galaxies of comparable stellar mass. J1608 has an anomalously high specific star-formation rate for its HI mass, similar to LyC-emitters Tololo 1247-232 and Haro 11. We constrain J1608's HI mass fraction ( f_ HI M_ HI /M_* ) to be 12.56 , falling below predicted values from optical and UV-derived scaling relations. J1608 is likely experiencing a brief period of intense star-formation.

gmw_00010977.name: Arp 302S
gmw_00010977.overview: Arp 302S is the face-on southern galaxy in an infrared-luminous pair [arXiv:2401.03117]. Its carbon monoxide emission extends over 10 kiloparsecs, and Spitzer observations indicate little to no active galactic nucleus activity [arXiv:2401.03117].

sources.summary: The paper describes Arp 302S as the face-on southern galaxy in an infrared-luminous pair, with CO extending 10 kpc and little Spitzer evidence for AGN activity.

quotes.quote: \ is an edge-on galaxy and Arp 302S is a face-on galaxy armus09 .
However, the CO emission extends over 23 kpc in \ and 10 kpc in Arp 302S, contrasting starbursts.
Spitzer found no or little evidence of significant Ne [ v ] emission from Arp 302S, indicating no significant AGN activity in the southern galaxy of the pair.

gmw_00010978.name: RS-235
gmw_00010978.overview: RS-235 is a massive, quenched galaxy at a redshift of z=2.993 [arXiv:1210.4299]. It features an old, metal-rich stellar population that likely formed rapidly around z~4, and it exhibits a relatively flat Sersic profile [arXiv:1210.4299].

sources.summary: The paper identifies RS-235 as a massive quenched galaxy at z=2.993, with an old, metal-rich stellar population, compact size, and relatively flat Sersic profile.

quotes.quote: RS-235 stood out in this dataset as having a 2D spectrum with a pronounced break at 1.6 m, a well-constrained photometric redshift of z 3 and rest-frame UVJ colors consistent with an old stellar population Wuy07 . Furthermore, RS-235 is not seen at 24, 100 or 160 \ although, due to its redshift and the depth of the maps, this non-detection only provides very loose constraints on its SFR, with an upper limit of SFR <400 M_ yr ^ 1 assuming recent templates from Mag12 .

As shown in Figure fig:sedfit , the stacked spectrum shows two distinct spectral features: the break at 1.6 m and an absorption feature (H _ ) at 1.636 m. Their position suggests a redshift of z 3 , which was measured by comparison with templates (see below), yielding z=2.993 0.015 . As its spectrophotometry precludes dusty SF solutions (see below), this makes RS-235 the most distant PEG confirmed so far.

RS-235 appears to be a quenched, if not entirely passive galaxy at z=2.99 , the most distant spectroscopically confirmed so far. Its spectrophotometric properties suggest that this galaxy formed the bulk of its stars relatively quickly at z 4 and already has a high metallicity, comparable to that of local ETGs e.g., Gal06 . With n 1.8 , it has a flatter profile than the typical passive population at lower redshifts and appears to have not acquired yet a characteristic ETG morphology.

gmw_00010979.name: SDSS J2313+0034
gmw_00010979.overview: SDSS J2313+0034 is a highly luminous quasar [arXiv:1906.04290]. Its host galaxy is unambiguously elevated from the star-forming main sequence, though its star formation rate remains constrained by an upper limit [arXiv:1906.04290].

sources.summary: The paper identifies SDSS J2313+0034 as the sample's most luminous quasar, setting a star-formation upper limit and finding one template result elevated from the main sequence.

quotes.quote: For SDSS J2313+0034, the most luminous quasar in the sample, the expected AGN contribution is higher than the ALMA measurement, and we set an upper limit to L_ 850 m due to star formation at the ALMA measurement.

The two SF non-detections could be located below the MS, but their upper limits are still consistent with SFRs on the MS. Interestingly, one of them is SDSS J2313+0034, the most luminous quasar in our sample.
Five quasar host are clearly located below the MS, while only SDSS J2313+0034 is unambiguously elevated from the MS.

gmw_00010980.name: PS J0030-1525
gmw_00010980.overview: PS J0030-1525 is a fold-quad gravitationally lensed system containing a quasar at a redshift of z=3.36 [arXiv:2206.04696]. The system exhibits anomalous flux ratios, which suggest a strong demagnification of one of its images [arXiv:2206.04696].

sources.summary: The paper describes PS J0030-1525 as a fold-quad lensed quasar discovered from Gaia/WISE data, with spectroscopic quasar images, anomalous flux ratios, and high magnification.

quotes.quote: PS J0030-1525 This lens was discovered by Lemon18 by cross-matching multiple catalogued detections in Gaia Data Release 1 (DR1) against photometric quasar candidates from the Wide-field Infrared Spectroscopic Explorer. The imaging in Pan-STARRS shows just two blue point sources offset from a galaxy, and follow-spectroscopy confirmed these to both be quasars at z=3.36. An archival VST-ATLAS band image revealed a likely counterimage, and Lemon18 suggested that this system is likely a fold quad, with image A composed of a merging pair. They modeled the system as an SIE + shear, predicting flux ratios of 7:7:3:1 (ABCD), yet only measuring 7:0.5:4:1, suggesting a strong demagnification of image B. They report a particularly large best-fit total model magnification of 71.

gmw_00010981.name: TN J1049-1258
gmw_00010981.overview: TN J1049-1258 is a radio galaxy located at a redshift of 3.697 [arXiv:2311.08507]. The galaxy exhibits a prodigious star formation rate of 4500 solar masses per year, contains a potential ionized gas outflow, and is accompanied by two nearby companion galaxies [arXiv:2311.08507].

sources.summary: The paper reports MUSE observations of TN J1049-1258, finding a z=3.7 radio galaxy with extended redshifted emission, possible outflow, prodigious star formation, and companions.

quotes.quote: Within this context we report results from VLT/MUSE integral field optical spectroscopy of TN J1049-1258, one of the most powerful radio sources known, at a redshift of 3.7. We detected extended ( 18 kpc) \ emission, spatially aligned with the radio axis, redshifted by 2250 60 \ with respect to the host galaxy systemic velocity, and co-spatial with UV continuum emission. This \ emission could arise from a companion galaxy, although there are arguments against this interpretation. Alternatively, it might correspond to an outflow of ionized gas stemming from the radio galaxy.

The outflow would be the highest redshift spatially resolved ionized outflow to date. The enormous amount of energy injected, however, appears to be unable to quench the host galaxy's prodigious star formation, occurring at a rate of 4500 M _ yr^ 1 , estimated using its far infra-red luminosity. Within the field we also found two companion galaxies at projected distances of 25 kpc and 60 kpc from the host, which suggests the host galaxy is harbored within a protocluster.
In this Letter we present the results obtained for TN J1049-1258, an HzRG at redshift 3.697 0.004 Bornancini_2007 , located at =10:49:06.2, = 12:58:19 (J2000).

gmw_00010982.name: G1
gmw_00010982.overview: G1 is a high-redshift galaxy located approximately 60 kiloparsecs southeast of the galaxy TN J1049-1258 [arXiv:2311.08507]. It is identified as a potential companion galaxy with an extended deconvolved size of 7.3 kiloparsecs [arXiv:2311.08507].

sources.summary: The paper identifies G1 as a potential companion to TN J1049-1258, located 60 kpc southeast, with similar-redshift emission, g-dropout photometry, and extended size.

quotes.quote: The brightest UV source in the field is another potential companion we named G1, located 8 ( 60 kpc) southeast of the host galaxy. Its \ emission was detected at a very similar redshift (see Fig. fig:compspec , top panel) and its high redshift identification is also supported by it being a g-dropout based on the photometry from Pan-STARRS Chambers_2016 . The Pan-STARRS aperture magnitudes for G1 are g=24.08 0.02, =22.44 0.01, and i=22.39 0.01, respectively, then satisfying the g-dropout criteria by pouliasis22 .
We must note, however, that G1 is also extended, with a deconvolved size of 7.3 0.5 kpc.

gmw_00010983.name: galaxy 36
gmw_00010983.overview: Galaxy 36 is a high-redshift infrared galaxy located at a redshift of approximately 2.66 [arXiv:1402.2686]. It contains unresolved dust continuum and carbon monoxide line emissions, with a massive estimated dynamical mass of 3 to 7 times 10^11 solar masses [arXiv:1402.2686].

sources.summary: The paper identifies galaxy 36 as the MIPS counterpart in LABd05, detecting unresolved dust and CO at its position, matching the Ly-alpha redshift, and estimating a massive dynamical mass.

quotes.quote: The dust continuum and CO lines are detected at the phase center, the position of the galaxy 36 (R.A. = 14 ^ h 34 ^ m 10 981 , decl. = + 33 17 32 48 ), which was identified from the deep optical and near-infrared HST observations as the most likely counterpart to the strong MIPS source Prescott12b . The dust continuum and CO line images are unresolved down to our synthesized beam (1.7 ; 13.5 kpc) and are coming from galaxy 36, or at least from the 7 kpc region around this position.
The CO line spectra show that the MIPS source (galaxy 36) is indeed located at the same redshift as the extended emission ( z 2.66).

A model-dependent mass estimate for the galaxy 36 is M_ dyn 5 ^2 R / G = 3--7 10^ 11 , where the range reflects the range of size estimates for the CO emitting region: the optical size of the galaxy 36 and the beam size of CO(5--4) observations as an upper limit ( R = 7 kpc). Higher resolution CO observations could yield a more accurate mass estimate by spatially resolving the velocity structure of the CO-emitting region and by determining the inclination of the galaxy.

gmw_00010984.name: SDSS J021647.53 011341.5
gmw_00010984.overview: SDSS J021647.53 011341.5 is a newly discovered double-peaked broad emission line quasar [arXiv:2203.13229]. Its broad emission line complex requires two broad Gaussian components to fit, making it a candidate supermassive black hole binary [arXiv:2203.13229].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J0216 as a newly discovered double-peaked broad-line quasar and candidate SMBH binary whose spectra require two broad Gaussian components.

quotes.quote: We report on the discovery of SDSS J021647.53 011341.5 (hereafter J0216) as a double-peaked broad emission line source.

Newly discovered candidate SMBH binary: J0216 As can be seen in Figure fitted_models , the broad emission line complex has a steeper blue than red wing. The attempt to fit the line complex with a fit-function containing only one broad emission line results in a poor fit, with a ^2_ red of 17.7 and 14.0 for region 1 and 2, respectively. The model has 10 degrees of freedom (DOF) in region 1, and 8 DOF in region 2. A model with two broad Gaussian components reduces the ^2_ red to 4.5 and 7.2 for region 1 and 2, respectively ( AIC = 377 and 225).

gmw_00010985.name: NGC5846-UDG1
gmw_00010985.overview: NGC5846-UDG1 is an ultra-diffuse galaxy that is dark-matter poor compared to standard stellar-to-halo-mass relations [arXiv:2304.14431]. Its globular cluster population exhibits strong radial mass segregation and has an initial spatial distribution more extended than the galaxy's smooth stellar distribution [arXiv:2304.14431].

sources.summary: The paper models NGC5846-UDG1's globular clusters to infer a dark-matter-poor, low-concentration halo and an initially extended GC spatial distribution.

quotes.quote: In the remarkable example of ultra-diffuse galaxy, NGC5846-UDG1, the GC population exhibits strong radial mass segregation, indicative of dynamical-friction-driven orbital decay, which opens the possibility of using imaging data alone to constrain the dark-matter content of the galaxy. To explore this possibility, we develop a semi-analytical model of GC evolution, which starts from the initial mass function, the initial structure-mass relation, and the initial spatial distribution of the GC progenitors, and follows the effects of dynamical friction, tidal evolution, and two-body relaxation. Using Markov Chain Monte Carlo, we forward-model the GCs in a NGC5846-UDG1-like potential to match the observed GC mass, size, and spatial distributions, and to constrain the profile of the host halo and the origin of the GCs. We find that, with the assumptions of zero mass segregation when the star clusters were born, NGC5846-UDG1 is dark-matter poor compared to what is expected from stellar-to-halo-mass relations, and its halo concentration is low, irrespective of having a cuspy or a cored halo profile. Its GC population has an initial spatial distribution more extended than the smooth stellar distribution.

gmw_00010986.name: RR 143
gmw_00010986.overview: RR 143 is an isolated galaxy pair consisting of an early-type galaxy and a late-type companion [arXiv:0905.1264, arXiv:astro-ph/0610116]. The system exhibits extended, asymmetric X-ray emission that is centered on the early-type galaxy and elongated toward its companion [arXiv:0905.1264].

sources.summary: The paper treats RR 143 as an X-ray-bright E+S pair, finds two new associated members, raises its membership to four galaxies, and notes extended asymmetric X-ray emission toward the late-type companion.

quotes.quote: We found two and ten new members likely to be associated with the X-ray bright systems RR 143 and RR 242, respectively. The X-ray faint RR 210 and RR 216, which were only partially covered by the VIMOS observations, have two and three new faint members, respectively. The new members increase the number of associated galaxies to 4, 7, 6, and 16 for RR 143, RR 210, RR 216, and RR 242, respectively, down to M_R 12 + 5 h_ 100 .

X-ray emission in RR 143 and RR 242 is centred on the elliptical but is much more extended than the optical light. The emission in RR 143, although centred on the elliptical, shows an asymmetric elongation towards the late-type companion. The total extension is 500 \ (90 h_ 100 ^ 1 kpc). The extended emission from RR 242 is more regular and has an extent of 700 \ (120 h_ 100 ^ 1 kpc). RR 210 and RR 216 show relatively faint and compact (i.e., within the optical galaxy) X-ray emission, consistent with an origin in an evolved stellar population. The emission in RR 143 and RR 242 can be argued to be related to a group potential (as in CPG127) rather than to an individual galaxy.

sources.summary: RR 143 is presented as an isolated NGC 2305/NGC 2307 E+S pair; NGC 2305 shows structural substructure and ambiguous lenticular-versus-elliptical properties.

quotes.quote: RR 143 (NGC 2305 + NGC 2307) involves a very isolated pair with projected component separation R 51 kpc and velocity difference V 270 km s ^ 1 . The much larger velocity difference implied by NED derives from an older and less accurate measurement for NGC 2307 (see Table table1 ). A study of the ellipticity profile for NGC 2305 ( RR96 ) shows that the outer regions of the galaxy ( 20 ) are on average flatter ( 0.3) than the inner ones ( 0.22-025) consistent with the presence of sub-structure. Isophotal shape analysis also supports the presence of sub-structure being boxy within 20 \ of the center and disky between 20 and 35 . A recent bulge/disk decomposition study DeSou04 suggests that the galaxy is lenticular rather than an elliptical.

gmw_00010987.name: RR 210
gmw_00010987.overview: RR 210 is a strongly interacting galaxy pair consisting of NGC 4105 and NGC 4106 [arXiv:astro-ph/0610116]. The system exhibits a disturbed optical morphology [arXiv:0905.1264], featuring perturbed arms or tails in NGC 4106 and a dusty center in NGC 4105 [arXiv:astro-ph/0610116].

sources.summary: The paper describes RR 210 as an X-ray-faint E+S pair with two new faint members, seven associated galaxies, compact stellar-population X-ray emission, and disturbed optical morphology tied to active poor-group evolution.

quotes.quote: We found two and ten new members likely to be associated with the X-ray bright systems RR 143 and RR 242, respectively. The X-ray faint RR 210 and RR 216, which were only partially covered by the VIMOS observations, have two and three new faint members, respectively. The new members increase the number of associated galaxies to 4, 7, 6, and 16 for RR 143, RR 210, RR 216, and RR 242, respectively, down to M_R 12 + 5 h_ 100 .

RR 210 and RR 216 show relatively faint and compact (i.e., within the optical galaxy) X-ray emission, consistent with an origin in an evolved stellar population. The emission in RR 143 and RR 242 can be argued to be related to a group potential (as in CPG127) rather than to an individual galaxy. In such a scenario, RR 210 and RR 216 could represent the active part of very poor and loose evolving groups see e.g., Rampazzo06 . The activity is reflected by the optically extended and distorted morphologies (see Paper III).

sources.summary: RR 210 is described as a strongly interacting NGC 4105/NGC 4106 pair with early-type colors, perturbed arms or tails in NGC 4106, and dusty/disky structure in NGC 4105.

quotes.quote: RR 210 (NGC 4105 + NGC 4106) form a strongly interacting pair AM87 separated by projected 7.7 kpc and 258 km s ^ 1 . It can also be described as a loose triplet Tully88 if one includes IC 764 with a similar recession velocity (2127 km s ^ 1 ) and a projected separation of 46.3 \ ( 363 kpc). RR96 obtained B, V and R surface photometry for both components which show almost constant color profiles (B-V) 0.9-1 suggesting that both are early-type galaxies. NGC 4105 shows no evidence of perturbation while NGC 4106 is strongly perturbed with arms/tails that are likely a product of interaction with the companion. NGC 4105 shows evidence for a dusty center and disky outer structure.

gmw_00010988.name: RR 216
gmw_00010988.overview: RR 216 is an isolated galaxy pair located on the outskirts of the Hydra-Centaurus region [arXiv:astro-ph/0610116]. The system consists of IC 3290, which features a well-developed bar and a grand-design spiral pattern, and NGC 4373, a disky elliptical galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0610116].

sources.summary: RR 216 is treated as an isolated IC 3290/NGC 4373 pair on the Hydra-Centaurus outskirts, with IC 3290 barred and spiral-patterned and NGC 4373 a disky elliptical.

quotes.quote: RR 216 (IC 3290 and NGC 4373) form an isolated pair according to both Sadler84 and AM87 with a projected separation of 26 kpc and V = 54 km s ^ 1 . The system is in the Hydra-Centaurus region but located on the outskirts at 4.9 ^ from the cluster center Dick86 . B, V, and R surface photometry RR96 shows (B-V) 1 throughout both galaxies although IC 3290 shows a well developed bar and a ``grand design'' spiral pattern. NGC 4373 shows disky structure in the isophotal shape profile but the luminosity profile is well represented by a ^ 1/4 law suggesting that the galaxy is a disky E.

gmw_00010989.name: RR 242
gmw_00010989.overview: RR 242 is an X-ray-bright galaxy pair consisting of an early-type elliptical and a late-type companion [arXiv:0905.1264, arXiv:astro-ph/0610116]. The system is situated within a loose group of sixteen associated galaxies [arXiv:0905.1264, arXiv:astro-ph/0610116] and exhibits regular, extended X-ray emission [arXiv:0905.1264].

sources.summary: The paper treats RR 242 as an X-ray-bright E+S pair with ten new associated members, sixteen galaxies total, regular extended X-ray emission, and a shell-bearing early-type member.

quotes.quote: We found two and ten new members likely to be associated with the X-ray bright systems RR 143 and RR 242, respectively. The X-ray faint RR 210 and RR 216, which were only partially covered by the VIMOS observations, have two and three new faint members, respectively. The new members increase the number of associated galaxies to 4, 7, 6, and 16 for RR 143, RR 210, RR 216, and RR 242, respectively, down to M_R 12 + 5 h_ 100 .

X-ray emission in RR 143 and RR 242 is centred on the elliptical but is much more extended than the optical light. The emission in RR 143, although centred on the elliptical, shows an asymmetric elongation towards the late-type companion. The total extension is 500 \ (90 h_ 100 ^ 1 kpc). The extended emission from RR 242 is more regular and has an extent of 700 \ (120 h_ 100 ^ 1 kpc). RR 210 and RR 216 show relatively faint and compact (i.e., within the optical galaxy) X-ray emission, consistent with an origin in an evolved stellar population. The emission in RR 143 and RR 242 can be argued to be related to a group potential (as in CPG127) rather than to an individual galaxy.

In Fig. 7, we display residual images after subtraction of a galaxy model constructed from the isophotal fit with ELLIPSE . Different galaxy substructures are clearly visible in the residual images including: asymmetries (RR143 09192, RR242 23187, RR242 25575), bars (RR143 09192, RR216 04052, RR242 22327), filaments (RR242 13326), and shells (RR242 24352). The system of shells in RR242 24352 extends to a radius of 6 h_ 100 ^ 1 kpc and is not aligned ( PA 30 ^ ) with the semi-major axis of the galaxy.

sources.summary: RR 242 is described as the NGC 5090/NGC 5091 pair in a richer loose-group environment; NGC 5090 is an elliptical FRI radio source and the pair has extended X-ray emission.

quotes.quote: RR 242 (NGC 5090 and NGC 5091) has parameters similar to the other pairs (R =17 kpc, V = 108 km s ^ 1 ), but it is found in a richer local environment than the previous systems. Available NED data suggest that the pair is part of a loose group. Four additional luminous galaxies with comparable redshifts can be found within a radius of 300 kpc around NGC 4105: NGC 5082 (at 5.8 \ /77.5 kpc, V _ pair = 421 km s ^ 1 ), NGC 5090A (at 20.3 \ /298.4 kpc, V _ pair = 45 km s ^ 1 ), NGC 5090B (at 13.8 \ /184.1 kpc, V _ pair = 773 km s ^ 1 ) and ESO 270 G007 (at 24.4 \ /326.1 kpc, V _ pair = 275 km s ^ 1 ).

NGC 5090 appears to be a``bona fide'' elliptical without signatures of interaction according to the surface photometry of Govo00 . The galaxy hosts an FRI radio source (PKS B1318-434) with two jets aligned perpendicular to a line connecting the galaxy nuclei see e.g. and references therein Lloyd96 . Carol93 have obtained the velocity dispersion and rotation velocity profiles of NGC 5090 which show high central velocity dispersion and low rotation velocity, both characteristic of E galaxies.

gmw_00010990.name: ESO 415-G26
gmw_00010990.overview: ESO 415-G26 is a peculiar S0 lenticular galaxy characterized by a central host and a polar ring [arXiv:0909.4020]. The system features outer debris, ripples, an asymmetric envelope, and neutral hydrogen aligned with the polar ring and outer shells [arXiv:0909.4020].

sources.summary: ESO 415-G26 is presented as a peculiar S0 polar-ring galaxy with outer debris, ripples, an asymmetric envelope, and H I aligned with the polar ring and shells.

quotes.quote: also known as ESO 415-G26, is a peculiar S0 galaxy Whitmore87, Iodice02a . It is characterized by a polar ring less extended than the central host galaxy in the optical band. Debris is present in the galaxy outskirts at a position angle that is intermediate between the major axis of the host galaxy and the polar ring. Whitmore87 reported observations of a ``nice set of ripples and an extended asymmetric envelope". In attempting to date the accretion event responsible for the formation of the ring, they estimated an age of 1-3 Gyr.

The HI map of this object was obtained with the VLA by vanGorkom87 , and vanGorkom97 noticed that the neutral hydrogen lies along the major axis of the polar ring, with some correlation between HI and outer shells.

gmw_00010991.name: NGC 5329
gmw_00010991.overview: NGC 5329 is a bright elliptical galaxy that serves as the dominant member of the poor galaxy cluster WBL 472 [arXiv:0909.4020]. Its ultraviolet emission is concentrated centrally in the far-ultraviolet, while its near-ultraviolet emission matches the optical morphology and shows no evidence of shells [arXiv:0909.4020].

sources.summary: NGC 5329 is a bright elliptical in WBL 472; GALEX finds optical-like NUV emission, central FUV emission, and no evidence of shells in the GALEX bands.

quotes.quote: NGC 5329 is a bright elliptical galaxy and the dominant member of a poor cluster of galaxies, WBL 472, consisting of six companion galaxies according to White99 .
Theureau07 did not detect HI in the direction of the NGC 5329 group.

GALEX data of NGC 5329 show that the distribution/morphology of its NUV emission is comparable to that of the optical image, while FUV emission is evident only in the central regions of the galaxy. This implies that the NUV emission originates in the same kind of stellar population. The FUV emission, which is more concentrated toward the nucleus, suggests that different type of hot stars contribute to the flux see also Rampazzo07 . NGC 5329 does not show evidence of shells in the GALEX bands.

gmw_00010992.name: Galaxy E
gmw_00010992.overview: Galaxy E is a quiescent post-starburst galaxy spectroscopically confirmed at a redshift of z=3.442 [arXiv:2311.04867]. It is the most massive member of the Cosmic Vine structure and exhibits a broad H-alpha emission line that suggests active galactic nucleus activity [arXiv:2311.04867].

sources.summary: The paper presents Galaxy E as the Cosmic Vine’s most massive quiescent member, spectroscopically confirmed at z=3.442, with post-starburst features and possible AGN-related broad H emission.

quotes.quote: Galaxy E was selected as a quiescent candidate by in the catalog. Recently, it was re-selected by using its specific star formation rate (sSFR) derived from SED fitting with JWST photometry ( z_ phot =3.53 0.12 ), and also selected by using the NUVUVJ diagram in . It is the most massive galaxy in the Cosmic Vine with a log (M_*/M_ )=10.95 0.03 , which is confirmed at z=3.442 with JWST/NIRSpec Prism spectroscopy (Fig. fig:spec ). Galaxy E is well detected with a H emission and a strong Balmer break. As no other lines are present in the spectrum, it appears to be a post-starburst galaxy (e.g., Chen_YM2019PSB,French2021PSB ).

The Bagpipes fitting of the NIRSpec spectrum shows negligible star-formation with an upper limit of SFR _ SED <0.4 M_ yr ^ 1 (95th quantile). The inferred SFH is relatively uncertain but suggests a quenching time at z 4 . The H emission of Galaxy E appears dominated by a broad component (FWHM =3696 324 km s ^ 1 ), which suggests AGN activity or blending of [ NII ] +H .

gmw_00010993.name: Q0302 223
gmw_00010993.overview: The DLA counterpart system Q0302 223 consists of two close, H-alpha-emitting galaxies [arXiv:1805.05960]. These galaxies have an estimated stellar mass of approximately 10 to the power of 9.5 solar masses and are likely interacting, though they lack visible tidal streams [arXiv:1805.05960].

sources.summary: The paper identifies the Q0302 223 DLA counterpart as two close H-alpha-emitting galaxies likely responsible for the Lyman absorption, possibly interacting but without detected tidal streams.

quotes.quote: The Q0302 223 Field: In this field we find two close galaxies at the position of the H- emission (3+4, see figure fig:label ) . These two galaxies had already been found previously as well as two other clumps (1+2) that are even closer to the quasar sight line. Since the later two (1+2) did not show any H- emission at the redshift of the absorber in the SINFONI IFU data, we assume that the galaxies responsible for the Lyman- absorption are objects 3 and 4. Due to their proximity we may assume that they are in an interaction with each other although we do not see any features that hint at some interaction like tidal streams.
For the DLA counterpart towards Q0302 223 we find log( M_*/ M _ ) = 9.5 0.2, which is consistent with christensen14 who found 9.65 0.08.

gmw_00010994.name: Q0452 1640
gmw_00010994.overview: Q0452 1640 is a candidate single rotating disk galaxy with a gas fraction of 56 percent [arXiv:1805.05960]. Its structure is characterized by elongated and compact components, which may represent an edge-on disk with two star-forming clumps, a dust-obscured region, or a tadpole-like galaxy [arXiv:1805.05960].

sources.summary: The paper describes the Q0452 1640 DLA counterpart as a single rotating disk candidate with elongated and compact HST components, possibly an edge-on or tadpole-like galaxy.

quotes.quote: The Q0452 1640 Field: This galaxy has the clear profile of a single rotating disc in the SINFONI data. The HST images of this target show an elongated and a compact object. This might hint at two merging objects but since we see a clear disc in the infrared and in H- there is a strong possibility for this object to be an edge-on disc (b/a=0.36) galaxy with two separate clumps of star formation or a patch of dust that absorbs the emission in the optical. elmegreen12 have investigated so-called tadpole galaxies that typically have a strongly off-centered region of star formation and an elongated intensity profile. The DLA counterpart that we find here might be an example for such a tadpole galaxy.
We derive gas fractions ranging from a few percent (in the case of the absorbing-galaxy in the field of Q1009 0026) to 56 percent (for Q0452 1640).

gmw_00010995.name: Q1009 0026
gmw_00010995.overview: Q1009 0026 is a massive, low-redshift galaxy that exhibits a clumpy spiral arm structure [arXiv:1805.05960]. It has a low neutral gas fraction of only a few percent [arXiv:1805.05960].

sources.summary: The paper describes the Q1009 0026 absorbing galaxy as a massive, low-redshift DLA counterpart with clumpy spiral structure and a gas fraction of only a few percent.

quotes.quote: The Q1009 0026 Field: The HST images show the presence of clumps and spiral arms. After quasar PSF subtraction, we also note a number of smaller objects at small impact parameters to the quasar (see figure fig:1009field ). We measured the magnitudes for these but due to their proximity to the quasar, the errors are large and we cannot get a constraint of the redshift of these clumps from SED fitting.

We derive gas fractions ranging from a few percent (in the case of the absorbing-galaxy in the field of Q1009 0026) to 56 percent (for Q0452 1640). Such gas fractions are in the low range of the typical values derived in z 2-3 galaxies by others (e.g. erb06gas,law09 ). make predictions of the neutral gas fraction as a function of stellar mass at z =0 (10 < M_* /M _ < 11.5). Our most massive galaxy (Q1009 0026) is the only one from our sample that falls into their mass range. It agrees with their predictions, although it falls into the lower range of the predicted neutral gas fraction.

gmw_00010996.name: J0048 + 0640
gmw_00010996.overview: J0048 + 0640 is a compact symmetric object [arXiv:2209.10760]. Very Long Baseline Interferometry observations reveal a double-component structure extended along the northeast-southwest direction, with the Gaia nucleus positioned between the two components [arXiv:2209.10760].

sources.summary: The paper classifies J0048 + 0640 as a young compact symmetric object, resolving two VLBI components, ruling out lensing, and finding no significant proper motion over 14 years.

quotes.quote: J0048 + 0640 The first VLBI image of this source was made at 5 GHz from a European VLBI Network (EVN) observation in 1996. It showed a double-component source extended along the northeast--southwest direction 1999AA...344...51P . The two components were separated by 3.8 mas, consistently with our 8.4-GHz imaging results.

Both the NE and SW components exhibit flat spectra and high brightness temperatures. The average T_ B is 7.0 1.3 10^ 10 K for NE and 4.6 3.9 10^ 10 K for SW, with the Gaia nucleus positioned in between. The possibility of gravitational lensing can be ruled out because the two components would violate the preservation of surface brightness see e.g. 2019A&A...630A.108S,2021MNRAS.507L...6C . Considering the source compactness, it is very unlikely to be a dual AGN, but this scenario is difficult to rule out with observations at other wavebands because of the insufficient resolution. The most likely explanation is that J0048 + 0640 is a CSO.

gmw_00010997.name: J0753 + 4231
gmw_00010997.overview: J0753 + 4231 is a one-sided core-jet quasar [arXiv:2209.10760]. It exhibits a double structure in both 2.3- and 8.4-GHz radio images, with a jet that has an estimated maximum proper motion of 0.018 milliarcseconds per year [arXiv:2209.10760].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J0753 + 4231 as a one-sided core-jet quasar, using Gaia position, spectral-index mapping, brightness temperatures, and VLBI proper motion to characterize its radio jet.

quotes.quote: J0753 + 4231 The source was included in the CSO candidate sample of CSOs observed in the northern sky (COINS) and VLBA Imaging and Polarimetry Survey (VIPS) 2000ApJ...534...90P,2007ApJ...658..203H,2016MNRAS.459..820T . In recent studies of the radio spectra of high- z quasars, this source was also identified as a candidate MHz-peaked spectrum source with _ peak < 1 GHz in the observer's frame e.g. 2013AstBu..68..262M,2021MNRAS.508.2798S . In our study, J0753 + 4231 shows a double structure in both the 2.3- and 8.4-GHz images.

The Gaia position and the spectral index map support that the source is a one-sided core--jet quasar rather than a CSO. The core should be located in the outermost NE2 component. The average brightness temperatures T_ B of NE1 and NE2 are 8.4 1.7 10^ 10 K and 1.4 0.4 10^ 10 K, respectively. We estimate the maximum proper motion of the jet to be 0.018 0.001 mas yr ^ 1 ( 1.8 0.1 ) using NE2 (core) as the reference point.

gmw_00010998.name: J1316 + 6726
gmw_00010998.overview: J1316 + 6726 is a high-redshift active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2209.10760]. Very Long Baseline Interferometry observations reveal a bright core with strong non-thermal emission and a weak, extended radio jet that exhibits a sharp bend [arXiv:2209.10760].

sources.summary: The paper includes J1316 + 6726 to test its high-redshift nature, finding a bright core, a weak sharply bent jet, strong nonthermal emission, and no significant outward motion.

quotes.quote: J1316 + 6726 The redshift of this source is from photometric measurements, but with a very high probability 95 , 2009ApJS..180...67R . Since the jet is well-separated from the core, we included it in our sample and tried to check its high- z nature by determining its jet proper motions following the method introduced by 2020MNRAS.497.2260A . In the VLBI images of this source, a weak and extended jet can be found at around 8 mas southeast of the bright core component, with a sharp bending at a projected distance of 60 pc from the core. The jet is only marginally detected at 3 available epochs, and we did not detect a significant (i.e. 3 ) outward motion due to the significant positional errors and the short time period. The mean T_ B of the core component is 13.3 5.5 10^ 10 K, indicating strong non-thermal emission.

gmw_00010999.name: J1421 0643
gmw_00010999.overview: J1421 0643 is a high-redshift blazar [arXiv:2209.10760] and radio-loud quasar located at a redshift of z=3.69 [arXiv:2007.03536]. The source features aligned parsec- and kiloparsec-scale jets [arXiv:2209.10760] that extend toward the northeast [arXiv:2007.03536, arXiv:2209.10760].

sources.summary: The paper studies PKS J1421-0643 as a z=3.69 radio-loud quasar whose resolved X-ray jet fits an iC-CMB model with modest magnetic field, Doppler factor, and viewing angle.

quotes.quote: We have presented new results from , \ and the \ for the z=3.69 radio-loud quasar , studying the core and extended jet. We find very steep radio spectra in the extended jet allowing, for the first time in an X-ray-jetted quasar, a maximum Lorentz factor for the radio-synchrotron-emitting electrons to be established. High-resolution radio mapping at lower frequencies would test the predicted spectral flattening to frequencies where the spectrum is expected to reflect that from particle acceleration. PKS J1421-0643's high redshift makes it a prime candidate for application of the iC-CMB model to the detected X-ray emission, extending about 4.5 arcsec from the core.

The measurement of an X-ray spectral index typical of radiation from newly accelerated particles is consistent with such an interpretation. We have demonstrated that the data follow the expectations of the model for a modest magnetic field strength of a few nT, Doppler factor of about 4, and viewing angle of about 15 ^ . Such parameters imply that the jet extends for about 130 kpc. Under the model, the electrons emitting the highest-energy radio emission need a few separate acceleration regions along the jet, roughly consistent with the level of knottiness and bending that is seen.

sources.summary: The paper identifies J1421 0643 as a prominent high-redshift blazar with aligned pc- and kpc-scale jets and three VLBI components moving northeast from the core.

quotes.quote: J1421 0643 This source shows a typical core--jet radio structure. The mean T_ B is 4.2 2.1 10^ 10 K. This source is a prominent high- z blazar. Previous studies have discovered large kpc-scale radio and X-ray jets extending towards the northeast of this nucleus 2020MNRAS.497..988W . Our VLBI images reveal the pc-scale jet structure, showing three jet components moving away from the core radially toward the northeast direction.
The position angle of the pc-scale jet is consistent with that of the kpc-scale jet (about 30 ). The estimated maximum jet proper motion is 0.15 mas yr ^ 1 ( 16 ).

gmw_00011000.name: J1939 1002
gmw_00011000.overview: J1939 1002 is a quasar with an emission redshift of 3.787 [arXiv:1412.4043] that exhibits a core-jet radio morphology [arXiv:2209.10760]. Its radio jet features strong Doppler boosting [arXiv:2209.10760], and its line of sight contains complex deuterium absorption systems [arXiv:1412.4043].

sources.summary: The paper describes J1939 1002 as a core-jet radio source with compact and distant jet components, strong Doppler boosting, and measured proper motions for inner jet features.

quotes.quote: J1939 1002 The VLBI morphology resembles a core--jet radio source. The two compact jet components lie close to the bright core, and a distant jet component (J1) is in the northeastern direction at a distance of about 22 mas. This distant feature shows a slight change in the position angle. It could possibly be explained by the rise of the Doppler-boosted region caused by the helical jet path, which is usually seen in flat-spectrum radio quasars (FSRQs) e.g. 2000A&A...361..529A,2004A&A...417..887H . However, the possibility of jet interaction with the surrounding interstellar medium (ISM) cannot be ruled out either .

The mean T_ B of this source is 21.4 6.7 10^ 10 K, which well exceeds the equipartition value and indicates strong Doppler boosting with 4 . Since the jet component J1 is weak and diffuse at most available epochs, we just measured proper motions of J2 and J3. The resulting jet proper motion is 0.014 0.001 mas yr ^ 1 for J2 and 0.005 0.001 mas yr ^ 1 for J3.

sources.summary: The paper identifies PKS1937-1009, also J193957-100241, as a z_em=3.787 quasar whose extensively observed spectrum contains deuterium absorption systems and newly resolved complex velocity structure.

quotes.quote: In this paper, which focuses on the absorber at z_ abs = 3.256 , we present a new and precise measurement of the deuterium abundance measured in the spectrum along the line of sight to PKS1937-1009 (1950) also known as J193957-100241 (J2000). It was identified as a quasar with emission redshift z_ em = 3.787 by Lanzetta:1991 . The presence of deuterium was previously identified in two independent absorption systems along the line of sight at z_ abs = 3.256 Crighton:2004 and z_ abs = 3.572 Burles:1998a . Since those measurements were published, the quasar has been observed extensively with both the Very Large Telescope (VLT) and the Keck Telescope, effectively increasing the observation time by almost an order of magnitude. As a consequence of the dramatically increased signal-to-noise, the new data presented in this paper reveals a far more complex velocity structure in the absorbing gas -- Crighton:2004 detected only two components whereas we now detect five.

gmw_00011001.name: HE 0413-4031
gmw_00011001.overview: HE 0413-4031 is a bright active galactic nucleus classified as a quasar [arXiv:2304.13763, arXiv:2401.18052]. Long-term monitoring of the source reveals that its UV Fe II emission line exhibits a longer reverberation time delay than its Mg II line [arXiv:2304.13763].

sources.summary: The paper monitors HE 0413-4031 with SALT, measuring Mg II and UV Fe II reverberation delays and finding the Fe II delay longer, with wavelength-resolved behavior suggesting kinematic effects.

quotes.quote: The two quasars, HE 0413-4031 and HE 0435-4312 were discovered in the Hamburg-ESO slitless survey wisotzki2000 . These relatively bright quasars, located at the intermediate redshift (z 1), were selected for long-term monitoring with the Southern Large African Telescope (SALT). For these two sources, as well as for the third quasar, CTS C30.10, the time delay of the Mg II line with respect to the continuum was already determined Czerny2019, Zajacek2020, zajacek2021 , and the wavelength-resolved delays for CTS C30.10 were obtained prince_CTS_2022 .

For HE 0413-4031, the difference between the mean values is larger -- the mean FeII time delay is by 5.15 longer than the MgII time delay. For this quasar, Javelin, ICCF (centroid and peak), zDCF, and the Bartels estimator support this trend.

For the quasar HE 0413, the wavy pattern is not fully reproduced. It is likely that there are some kinematic effects that are not included in this simple picture. The mean relative shift of the Fe II and Mg II determined from the spectral fitting ( 1595 74 km s ^ 1 ) also implies such an effect.

sources.summary: The paper uses HE 0413-4031 as an example bright quasar, combining 32 SALT spectra and fitting Mg II and Fe II emission to test theoretical templates.

quotes.quote: HE 0413-4031 This quasar has been observed with the Southern African Large Telescope (SALT) for 13 years with the aim of performing the reverberation mapping of the Mg II line and the UV Fe II zajacek2020,prince2023,zajacek2023 . The obtained spectra cover a narrow range around the Mg II line but the spectral shape did not show any strong variability apart from the normalization of the spectrum. We thus constructed the mean spectrum of this quasar by combining all 32 available data sets for a better S/N ratio. The shape of the Mg II line in this source is particularly simple, well fitted as a single kinematic component of the Lorentzian shape. We fit all the parameters together (slope and normalization of the power law, normalization and the broadening of the Fe II template, the position, normalization and FWHM of the Mg II) since in case of such a short data range we do not have any possibility to find parts of the spectrum unaffected by Fe II and fit first the underlying power law.

The narrow spectral range makes the fits not strongly constraining the Fe II templates but the fits are very satisfactory. The results of the spectral fitting are shown in Figure fig:HE_0413 . The best solution corresponds to _ H ( cm^ 2 s^ 1 ) = 18.25 , shown in the middle panel of Figure fig:HE_0413 .

gmw_00011002.name: SDSS J141352.99+523444.2
gmw_00011002.overview: SDSS J141352.99+523444.2 is a high signal-to-noise reverberation-mapping quasar [arXiv:2401.18052]. It is classified as a moderate iron emitter with a moderate Eddington ratio and a weak contribution from forbidden oxygen emission lines [arXiv:2401.18052].

sources.summary: The paper selects SDSS J141352.99+523444.2 as a high-S/N reverberation-mapping quasar, combines 70 spectra, and measures Fe II strength and Eddington ratio for template testing.

quotes.quote: SDSS RM102 As a second example, we selected the quasar SDSS J141352.99+523444.2 (SDSS RM102) from the SDSS Reverberation Mapping (SDSS-RM) catalogue shen2019 considering a high signal-to-noise, moderate Fe II emitter ( R_ FeII >0.5 ), and a reliable time lag for H or Mg II 2800 (maximum Pearson correlation coefficient >0.4 ). The spectrum of the source covers a broad wavelength range (2000-5500 ). This allowed us to cover both the rest-frame optical Fe II as well as UV Fe II and to test the feasibility of using the same Fe II template to fit the entire UV-to-optical spectral range. The size of its BLR region has been estimated by different methods, in this paper we took the one estimated by the Interpolated Cross-Correlation Function (ICCF), _ MgII-ICCF =101.7^ +11.6 _ +10.3 and _ H ICCF =104.6^ +20.5 _ +18.5 days in the observed-frame shen2023 . Using the SDSS Science Archive Server (SAS) from the DR16 https://dr16.sdss.org/optical/spectrum/search , we found 70 spectroscopic epochs observed during four years.

The new spectral fitting indicates a R_ FeII =1.24, which is approximately twice the value reported by shen2019 . On the other hand, this source shows a moderate Eddington ratio _ Edd =0.329 shen2019 . The weak contribution of the doublet [ OIII ] 5007,4959 suggests that RM102 has a moderate Eddington ratio and is close to the xA sources marziani2023 .

gmw_00011003.name: FCC 113
gmw_00011003.overview: FCC 113 is a late-type, star-forming dwarf spiral galaxy located in a low-density region of its cluster [arXiv:1906.08187]. It exhibits a lopsided morphology, smooth stellar rotation, low stellar velocity dispersion, and extended ionized-gas emission powered by massive stars [arXiv:1906.08187].

sources.summary: FCC 113 is described as a late-type spiral and star-forming dwarf with lopsided morphology, smooth stellar rotation, low dispersion, and extended ionised gas.

quotes.quote: FCC 113 (ESO 358-G015) -- Fig. fig:FCC113map FCC 113 is a late-type spiral on the north-western side of the cluster at a distance of R_ proj =1.3^ from FCC 213 (NGC 1399). It is one of the intermediate infallers residing in the low-density region. It was classified as a star-forming dwarf galaxy by Drinkwater2001 . Its morphology resembles that of a lopsided spiral as confirmed by the smooth stellar velocity map. The maximum rotation velocity is v_ max 50 km s ^ 1 at R 20 arcsec, while the stellar velocity dispersion is low ( 15 km s ^ 1 ) and constant at all radii.

This galaxy shows extended ionised-gas emission powered by massive stars consistent with the previous findings from Drinkwater2001 , with several localised regions where star-formation is more intense.

gmw_00011004.name: FCC 176
gmw_00011004.overview: FCC 176 is a luminous barred late-type galaxy characterized by a bar and an outer ring [arXiv:1906.08187]. It is the only late-type galaxy in the Fornax 3D sample that does not show ionized-gas emission, which may be due to ram-pressure stripping [arXiv:1906.08187].

sources.summary: FCC 176 is described as a luminous barred late-type galaxy with an outer ring, possible ram-pressure quenching, regular stellar rotation, and arm/bar dispersion structure.

quotes.quote: FCC 176 (NGC 1369) -- Fig. fig:FCC176map FCC 176 is one of the most luminous LTGs of the F3D sample and is characterised by a prominent bar and an outer ring. It is located at R_ proj =0.9^ from the centre on the south-western side of the cluster. It is an intermediate infaller in the transion between the high and low-density regions of the cluster where the X-ray emission is still present. Ram-pressure stripping could have acted to stop star formation once the galaxy entered into the cluster core. FCC 176 is indeed the only LTG of the F3D sample that does not show ionised-gas emission.

The stellar velocity map shows a regular rotation with a maximum velocity v_ max 120 km s ^ 1 at R 30 arcsec. The stellar velocity dispersion map is characterised by smaller values ( 30 km s ^ 1 ) for locations on the spiral arm on the north-western side of the galaxy and larger values along the bar ( 60 km s ^ 1 ).

gmw_00011005.name: J140244.51+155956.6
gmw_00011005.overview: J140244.51+155956.6 is a changing-look blazar that was originally classified as a BL Lacertae object [arXiv:2103.10861]. Its LAMOST and SDSS spectra show intense [O II], H, and [O III] doublet emission lines, leading to its reclassification as a flat-spectrum radio-loud quasar [arXiv:2103.10861].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J140244.51+155956.6 as a changing-look blazar whose LAMOST and SDSS spectra show strong emission lines, leading to BZQ classification.

quotes.quote: The five potential changing-look blazars for which we found spectra are: J140244.51+155956.6, originally classified BZB baldwin77 , in Figure fig:clb , we show the literature spectrum baldwin77 , in comparison with that collected from LAMOST (2013 February11) and SDSS (2007 June 21). Those spectra available in both SDSS and LAMOST shows intense [ O _ II ] , H and the [ O _ III ] doublet emission lines letting us classify it as a BZQ.
