This suggests that it formed from the pre-enriched gas of the galactic components of HCG 16, this high metallicity content being a fundamental characteristic defining TDGs.

Assuming that the object is dynamically stable and able to survive in the future, its fading in time via the aging of its stellar component will make it undetectable in optical observations in just 2 Gyr of evolution, even in the deepest current or future optical surveys. Its high H i mass, M(HI) = 3.9 10 ^8 M _ , and future undetectable stellar component will make the object match the observational properties of dark galaxies, that is, dark matter halos that failed to turn gas into stars.

gmw_00011484.name: ES1C 9
gmw_00011484.overview: ES1C 9 is a high-redshift galaxy with a photometric redshift of approximately 3.0 [arXiv:1807.03324]. It exhibits an irregular morphology featuring a compact core with diffuse emission to its south, and it may be a late-stage merger [arXiv:1807.03324].

sources.summary: The paper identifies ES1C 9 as a zphot=3.0 galaxy in the ES1C H1 Herschel source, with irregular morphology, high-redshift far-infrared behavior, weak radio detection, and possible disturbed-merger character.

quotes.quote: ES1C H1 This source (Figure fig:ES1C-H1 ) has three 24 m sources apparently associated with it, which are in turn associated with galaxies ES1C 9, ES1C 13, and ES1C 15. ES1C 9 ( z_ phot =3.0 ) appears to have an irregular morphology, with a compact core and more diffuse emission to its south (Figure fig:es1c , bottom left inset). It is brighter at 350 m than 250 m (Figure fig:seds1 ), and barely detected at 10 GHz, consistent with its relatively high redshift.

With only a few objects it is difficult to estimate a merger rate, but of the four Herschel sources we have examined, we have one close pair (XMM-C2 69), one candidate triple AGN (ES1C 60, 62, and 55), and two objects at different redshifts associated with the same Herschel source with disturbed morphologies that might be late stage mergers (ES1C 9 and ES1C 15).

gmw_00011485.name: ES1C 15
gmw_00011485.overview: ES1C 15 is a disturbed-looking galaxy at a photometric redshift of 2.3 [arXiv:1807.03324]. It is detected at 10 GHz, dominates the 250 micron flux, and may be a late-stage merger [arXiv:1807.03324].

sources.summary: The paper describes ES1C 15 as a disturbed zphot=2.3 galaxy that dominates the 250 micron flux, is detected at 10 GHz, and may be a late-stage merger.

quotes.quote: ES1C 15 ( z_ phot =2.3 ) is another disturbed looking galaxy, possibly in a group (though ES1C 12, the most nearby candidate companion, has a lower photometric redshift of 1.6). ES1C 15 dominates the 250 m flux and it is detected at 10 GHz (Figure fig:es1c , bottom right and Figure fig:ES1C-H1 ). The peaks of both the infrared and radio emission are offset to the South-East of the stellar light by about 0 5 . In the HerMES catalog, one source is between ES1C 13 and ES1C 15 (4HERMES S250 SF J003519.2-440145) and one between ES1C 9 and ES1C 15 (4HERMES S250 SF J003520.4-440151).

With only a few objects it is difficult to estimate a merger rate, but of the four Herschel sources we have examined, we have one close pair (XMM-C2 69), one candidate triple AGN (ES1C 60, 62, and 55), and two objects at different redshifts associated with the same Herschel source with disturbed morphologies that might be late stage mergers (ES1C 9 and ES1C 15).

gmw_00011486.name: ES1C 55
gmw_00011486.overview: ES1C 55 is a radio-loud active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1807.03324] that is classified as a quiescent galaxy [arXiv:1807.03324]. It has a compact host galaxy [arXiv:1807.03324] and is accompanied by a close companion galaxy, ES1C 56 [arXiv:1807.03324].

sources.summary: The paper identifies ES1C 55 as a radio-loud AGN with a companion, a similar redshift to ES1C 60 and ES1C 62, and compact/quiescent host-galaxy properties.

quotes.quote: A third source, ES1C 55 (R.A.(J2000) = 00:35:18.73, Dec.(J2000) = - 44:00:57.3), is associated with a relatively strong radio source ( S_ 8.4 GHz =175 5 Jy), but has no visible 24 m emission and is most likely a radio loud AGN. It has a close companion galaxy (ES1C 56). Finally, there is also a radio source very close ( 2 ^ SE) to the guide star (unfortunately too close to be able to obtain reliable optical/infrared photometry). Both ES1C 60 and ES1C 62, which appear as a close pair on the sky, have SEDs consistent with a dominant AGN component. ES1C 62 has a point source contribution approximately equal in flux to that of the extended host galaxy, so it is most likely a Type-1 (unobscured) AGN. All three AGN (ES1C 55, ES1C 60 and ES1C 62) have similar photometric redshifts (1.37, 1.45 and 1.44 for ES1C 55, ES1C 60 and ES1C 62, respectively, and ES1C 56, the non-AGN companion to ES1C 55, is at a redshift of 1.49.)

Also as expected, the Herschel galaxies fall on the red side of the star-forming population, and the radio galaxy (ES1C 55) plots amongst the quiescent objects. The AGN hosts (ES1C 55,60 and 62) also tend to have more compact scale sizes than the star-forming galaxies, a trend that has also been noted for high redshift AGN selected from WISE (Farrah et al. \ 2017).

gmw_00011487.name: ES1C 60
gmw_00011487.overview: ES1C 60 is a high-redshift Type-2 active galactic nucleus host galaxy with a photometric redshift of 1.45 [arXiv:1807.03324]. It forms a close pair with the nearby active galaxy ES1C 62, and both systems exhibit energy distributions dominated by an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1807.03324].

sources.summary: The paper presents ES1C 60 as an AGN-dominated member of a close ES1C 60/62 pair, with similar redshift to nearby AGN and compact high-redshift host properties.

quotes.quote: Two are associated with a relatively strong 24 m source, ES1C 60 and ES1C 62, and also with weak radio sources, so we assume these are the dominant contributors to the Herschel flux.

Both ES1C 60 and ES1C 62, which appear as a close pair on the sky, have SEDs consistent with a dominant AGN component. ES1C 62 has a point source contribution approximately equal in flux to that of the extended host galaxy, so it is most likely a Type-1 (unobscured) AGN. All three AGN (ES1C 55, ES1C 60 and ES1C 62) have similar photometric redshifts (1.37, 1.45 and 1.44 for ES1C 55, ES1C 60 and ES1C 62, respectively, and ES1C 56, the non-AGN companion to ES1C 55, is at a redshift of 1.49.)

This object, and its companion Type-2 AGN host ES1C 60 (which has a very similar luminosity and a half-light radius of 0 1 ), can be compared to the z 1.8 faint quasars studied with the NICMOS instrument on HST by Ridgway et al. \ (2001). In general, the host galaxies in the Ridgway et al. \ sample are fainter ( M_i 21 to 22 compared to M_i 23.3 ), but have larger scale sizes ( 2 kpc compared to 0.4-0.8 kpc).

gmw_00011488.name: ES1C 62
gmw_00011488.overview: ES1C 62 is a compact disk galaxy at a photometric redshift of 1.44 [arXiv:1807.03324]. It hosts a Type-1 active galactic nucleus and forms a close pair on the sky with ES1C 60 [arXiv:1807.03324].

sources.summary: The paper treats ES1C 62 as a Type-1 AGN in a close pair, decomposes its point-source nucleus and compact disk host, and compares its host to faint quasar hosts.

quotes.quote: Both ES1C 60 and ES1C 62, which appear as a close pair on the sky, have SEDs consistent with a dominant AGN component. ES1C 62 has a point source contribution approximately equal in flux to that of the extended host galaxy, so it is most likely a Type-1 (unobscured) AGN. All three AGN (ES1C 55, ES1C 60 and ES1C 62) have similar photometric redshifts (1.37, 1.45 and 1.44 for ES1C 55, ES1C 60 and ES1C 62, respectively, and ES1C 56, the non-AGN companion to ES1C 55, is at a redshift of 1.49.)

We used the Tractor to decompose the light from the Type-1 AGN ES1C 62 in the Gemini K band data (Figure fig:agn ), the only AGN to show a point source nucleus. Two components were fit, a point-source nucleus representing the AGN, and an extended host galaxy. The best fit indicates the AGN and the host galaxy each contribute 18 4 Jy to the total flux of the object, with the host being best fit by a fairly compact disk galaxy ( 0 05 0.02 half-light radius).

gmw_00011489.name: ESO 306 - IG 001
gmw_00011489.overview: ESO 306 - IG 001 is an interacting galaxy pair with a separation of approximately 20 arcseconds [arXiv:2211.04478]. Its southern nucleus hosts an active galactic nucleus that developed broad optical emission lines between 2016 and 2019 [arXiv:2211.04478].

sources.summary: The paper identifies the southern nucleus of ESO 306 - IG 001 as an interacting-pair AGN that developed broad optical emission between 2016-2019, with tentative BAT brightening.

quotes.quote: ESO 306 - IG 001 ESO 306 - IG 001 (BAT 280) is an interacting galaxy pair, separated by around 20 arcsec. We have verified that all the spectra used in our analysis are of the southern, active galaxy nucleus, which is clearly identifiable by virtue of its bluer colours in optical imaging and redder colours in WISE. In 2019 September, we identified the appearance of broad \ emission which was not present in 2016 March, and moreover the appearance of broad \ emission which was not present in 2017 July. The BAT light-curve shows tentative evidence for an increase in flux in the second half of 2017, during the last 6 months of currently available data.

gmw_00011490.name: UGC 03601
gmw_00011490.overview: UGC 03601 is classified as a changing-look active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2211.04478]. Its broad Balmer emission lines weakened significantly after 1999, coinciding with a drop in its X-ray flux that remained low through 2018 [arXiv:2211.04478].

sources.summary: The paper classifies UGC 03601 as a changing-look AGN whose broad Balmer emission weakened after 1999 while the BAT X-ray flux dropped and stayed low.

quotes.quote: UGC 03601 UGC 03601 (BAT 349) shows emission from broad \ and \ lines in 1999. In 2008 and 2018, emission from broad \ is significantly weaker, and no broad component is visible in . The BAT light-curve shows the X-ray flux dropping by a factor of 3 from 2006 to 2008, and staying at a lower level through to the end of the 157-month survey in 2018.

gmw_00011491.name: CGMW 5-04382
gmw_00011491.overview: CGMW 5-04382 is classified as a turn-on changing-look active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2211.04478]. It exhibited a brightening of broad line emission between 2010 and 2016, and historical observations suggest it was previously X-ray obscured [arXiv:2211.04478].

sources.summary: The paper classifies CGMW 5-04382 as a turn-on changing-look AGN with strengthening broad lines, prior X-ray obscuration, and noisy low-flux BAT behavior.

quotes.quote: CGMW 5-04382 CGMW 5-04382 (LEDA 2808003; BAT 981) shows a brightening of broad line emission in both \ and \ between 2010 April (when it displayed only narrow emission lines) and 2014 June, and again between 2014 June and 2016 July. We therefore classify it as a turn-on CL AGN. X-ray spectral analysis by 2017ApJS..233...17R found a column density of N_ H =10^ 23.2 , suggesting this source was X-ray obscured when the source was observed by Swift XRT in 2007 September. The BAT light-curve is noisy with low flux rates, which is consistent with the possibility that the CL transition observed in this source is due to variable obscuration.

gmw_00011492.name: 2MASX J19263018+4133053
gmw_00011492.overview: 2MASX J19263018+4133053 is an active galactic nucleus that resembled a type 2 AGN in May 2010, showing only narrow emission lines [arXiv:2211.04478]. By August 2015, it developed a broad base in its H-alpha line and was later detected as an optically variable source [arXiv:2211.04478].

sources.summary: The paper reports 2MASX J19263018+4133053 changing from type-2-like narrow-line spectra to a broad-line H-alpha base and later optical variability.

quotes.quote: 2MASX J19263018+4133053 2MASX J19263018+4133053 (LEDA 2182842; BAT 1037) resembled a type 2 AGN in 2010 May, with only narrow \ and \ emission lines present. In 2015 August, a broad base is clearly visible in the \ line, though a corresponding feature is not detected in \ in either 2015 August or 2018 March. 2MASX J19263018+4133053 was detected in 2020 and 2021 as a variable source in optical photometry (ZTF20aazwurf; AT 2020kcu; 2020TNSTR1365....1F ).

gmw_00011493.name: A
gmw_00011493.overview: Galaxy A is an embedded cluster radio galaxy located 70 arcseconds in projection from its cluster center [arXiv:0905.3552]. It features a bright radio core coincident with its optical counterpart, alongside a diffuse region extending to the northwest that exhibits a steepening spectral index [arXiv:0905.3552].

sources.summary: The paper identifies A as an embedded cluster radio galaxy with a likely core, diffuse northwest extension, steepening spectrum, and an optical counterpart coincident with the radio core.

quotes.quote: Using 8.460 GHz observations we have identified one such embedded radio galaxy, labelled A in Fig. fig:radioX and shown in a close-up view in the side panel of the same figure. This source is 70 '' away from the cluster center in projection. It is consists of a bright region, likely the core of the radio galaxy, with a spectral index of 0.7 between 1.465 GHz and 4.885 GHz, and an extended, more diffuse region extending toward the NW. The spectral index steepens up to 2 with increasing distance from the core. The optical counterpart coincides with the radio core (Fig. fig:radioX ) .

gmw_00011494.name: B
gmw_00011494.overview: B is a radio galaxy located southeast of the X-ray center of the MACS J0717 cluster [arXiv:0905.3552]. It is aligned with a large-scale optical and X-ray filament, and its spectroscopic redshift is consistent with the cluster itself [arXiv:0905.3552].

sources.summary: The paper describes B as one of two additional radio galaxies southeast of the cluster X-ray center, aligned with the large-scale filament and spectroscopically consistent with MACS J0717.

quotes.quote: Moving out from the cluster center, two additional radio galaxies, labeled B and C in Fig. fig:radioX , are visible at all the observed frequencies. Both are located to the SE of the cluster's X-ray center, at projected distances of 180 '' and 335 '' , respectively. We note their position along the X-ray and optical large-scale filament detected by Ebeling et al. \ (2004). Source C is the brightest cluster galaxy of the next cluster (also detected in the Chandra observation) that is going to merge with MACS J0717. In Table tab:sourcesPol we report the main radio properties of the identified radio galaxies at the observed frequencies. The optical counterparts of A, B, and C are all spectroscopically confirmed to lie at redshifts consistent with that of MACS J0717 proper.

gmw_00011495.name: C
gmw_00011495.overview: Galaxy C is a radio galaxy located southeast of the cluster center of MACS J0717 [arXiv:0905.3552]. It serves as the brightest cluster galaxy of an infalling cluster that is preparing to merge with MACS J0717 [arXiv:0905.3552].

sources.summary: The paper describes C as a radio galaxy southeast of the cluster center and the brightest cluster galaxy of an infalling cluster, with a redshift consistent with MACS J0717.

quotes.quote: Moving out from the cluster center, two additional radio galaxies, labeled B and C in Fig. fig:radioX , are visible at all the observed frequencies. Both are located to the SE of the cluster's X-ray center, at projected distances of 180 '' and 335 '' , respectively. We note their position along the X-ray and optical large-scale filament detected by Ebeling et al. \ (2004).

Source C is the brightest cluster galaxy of the next cluster (also detected in the Chandra observation) that is going to merge with MACS J0717. In Table tab:sourcesPol we report the main radio properties of the identified radio galaxies at the observed frequencies. The optical counterparts of A, B, and C are all spectroscopically confirmed to lie at redshifts consistent with that of MACS J0717 proper.

gmw_00011496.name: FDF-6024
gmw_00011496.overview: FDF-6024 is a high-redshift starburst galaxy located at a redshift of z = 2.372 [arXiv:astro-ph/0605081]. Its spectrum reveals asymmetric interstellar absorption profiles with two distinct velocity components whose relative strengths depend on the total line strength [arXiv:astro-ph/0605081].

sources.summary: The paper analyzes FDF-6024 as a z = 2.372 high-redshift starburst galaxy with narrower asymmetric interstellar absorption profiles and line-strength-dependent velocity components.

quotes.quote: Since there exist only few spectra of high-redshift starburst galaxies with a spectral resolution comparable with the present data, we used our composite spectrum (Fig. fig:mall_pap ) and the individual spectra of FDF-5903 at z = 2.774 and FDF-6024 at z = 2.372 (Fig. fig:5903_6024 ) to derive some characteristic properties of selected absorption lines for our sample of high-redshift galaxies. For the two galaxies FDF-5903 and FDF-6024 with a continuum S/N > 10 we investigated the strength and profiles of selected stellar and interstellar absorption lines in the individual spectra.

The interstellar lines in the spectrum of FDF-6024 show narrower profiles (total width about 430 km s ^ 1 ) with only two obvious components, one corresponding to approximately the systemic velocity and one at 190 km s ^ 1 . The profiles are again asymmetric and extend blueward to 400 km s ^ 1 . In contrast to FDF-5903 where the component at 156 km s ^ 1 always dominates, in the spectrum of FDF-6024 the relative strengths of two interstellar line components appear to depend on the total line strength. For weaker lines (Fe II 1608, Si II 1527) the low-velocity component is dominant, while for strong lines (C IV resonance doublet, Al II 1671) the blueshifted components appear stronger.

gmw_00011497.name: J0313--1806
gmw_00011497.overview: J0313--1806 is a luminous broad-absorption line quasar located at a redshift of 7.642 [arXiv:2101.03179]. The system hosts a massive black hole driving a strong active galactic nucleus outflow, and its host galaxy is undergoing intense star formation [arXiv:2101.03179].

sources.summary: The paper reports J0313--1806 as a z = 7.642 luminous BAL quasar with a massive black hole, AGN outflow, and an intensely star-forming ALMA-detected host galaxy.

quotes.quote: We report the discovery of a luminous quasar at z=7.642 , J0313 1806, the most distant quasar yet known. This quasar has a bolometric luminosity of 3.6 10^ 13 L_ . Deep spectroscopic observations reveal a SMBH with a mass of (1.6 0.4) 10^9M_ in this quasar. The existence of such a massive SMBH just 670 million years after the Big Bang challenges significantly theoretical models of SMBH growth.

In addition, the quasar spectrum exhibits strong broad absorption line (BAL) features in C 4 and Si 4 , with a maximum velocity close to 20 of the speed of light. The relativistic BAL features, combined with a strongly blueshifted C 4 emission line, indicate that there is a strong active galactic nucleus (AGN) driven outflow in this system. ALMA observations detect the dust continuum and [ C 2 ] emission from the quasar host galaxy, yielding an accurate redshift of 7.6423 0.0013 and suggesting that the quasar is hosted by an intensely star-forming galaxy, with a star formation rate of 200 M_ yr^ 1 and a dust mass of 7 10^7 M_ .

gmw_00011498.name: J1325+0412
gmw_00011498.overview: J1325+0412 is a type 2 active galactic nucleus with a spectral redshift of 0.73 [arXiv:2306.03357]. It is a candidate dual or off-nucleus active galactic nucleus system that may exhibit Gaia jitter and flux variability [arXiv:2306.03357].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J1325+0412 as a type 2 AGN target and discusses whether Gaia jitter could indicate a dual or off-nucleus AGN system.

quotes.quote: Note one of the targets, J1325+0412, is a type 2 AGN with a spectral redshift of 0.73.

Further note one of our candidate J1325+0412 is indeed a type 2 AGN. If it is in a dual AGN system, its jitter and flux variability measured with Gaia shall be caused by the other unobscured AGN in the system (which however was not seen in SDSS spectrum), unless itself changed to type 1 during Gaia observations. Followup spectroscopic observation is required to verify if this sources is a changing-look quasar e.g. LaMassa2015 in dual or off-nucleus AGN system.

gmw_00011499.name: J1433+4842
gmw_00011499.overview: J1433+4842 is an active galactic nucleus and a candidate dual or off-nucleus quasar [arXiv:2306.03357]. It exhibits a statistically weak Gaia-radio offset that may be artificially affected by a nearby Gaia source located within 1.09 arcseconds [arXiv:2306.03357].

sources.summary: The paper treats J1433+4842 as the candidate with a statistically weak Gaia-radio offset, likely affected by a nearby Gaia source but still relevant to dual/off-nucleus quasar tests.

quotes.quote: All but one of five candidates (J1433+4842) have normalized separations > 24, reaching a confidence level of 1 - 10^ 125 . On the other hand, for J1433, considering the 1 confidence range of the best-fit positional angle, its normalized separation could be as low as 1.83 (corresponding to a confidence level of 81.3 ). It indicates that the Gaia-radio offset of J1433+4842 is relatively small and statistically insignificant. We found there is an extra source in Gaia EDR3 near J1433+4842 within 1.09 .

The non-zero ipd frac multi peak of J1433+4842 supports this scenario as well. Therefore, although can not be ruled out (see further discussion in sec:dis ), the observed jittering of J1433+4842 is possibly artificial due to the contamination of the nearby source.

gmw_00011500.name: J1733+5520
gmw_00011500.overview: J1733+5520 is an unresolved quasar that exhibits a large Gaia multi-peak fraction [arXiv:2306.03357]. It displays an artificial proper motion that is likely caused by source jittering [arXiv:2306.03357].

sources.summary: The paper highlights J1733+5520 as the only target with a large Gaia multi-peak fraction, an unresolved separation range, and significant artificial proper motion from jittering.

quotes.quote: Among our five targets, only J1733+5520 shows a large ipd frac multi peak of 20 . As J1733+5520 is unresolved by Gaia, its possible separation is likely between 0.11 \ (Gaia's PSF) and 0.35 \ Mannucci_2022 , consistent with the range of 0.028-0.28 \ we derived based on its Gaia-radio offset. Moreover, selected abnormal Gaia unresolved quasars (e.g. quasar pairs. yielded a sample of 152 quasars with excess proper motions, which were candidates of dual/multiple AGNs, or lensed quasars. Note two of our candidates (J1733+5520 and J2109+0656) do exhibit highly significant Gaia DR3 proper motions, with covariance-normalized values of 16.0 and 11.8 respectively, and J2109+0656 has an uncertainty-normalized parallax of +4.3. Such proper motion and parallax should be artificial due to the jittering of the sources.

gmw_00011501.name: q0109
gmw_00011501.overview: The object q0109 is a high-redshift quasar host galaxy [arXiv:0909.2630]. Observations of its extended residual light suggest that the host may be a large exponential disk galaxy with a mass equivalent to a 5L* galaxy today [arXiv:0909.2630].

sources.summary: The paper identifies q0109 as a likely z=4 quasar host, measuring extended residual light consistent with a large exponential disk and a present-day-equivalent 5L* galaxy.

quotes.quote: For q0109, the residuals add up to m_K^ Vega (obs) 22.7 and they extend to a diameter of approximately 2 . We use Fig. fig-isod a (the 22.4 depth is appropriate for this image) to learn that this galaxy could for example be a large scale-length exponential disk. Locating this curve on Fig. fig-isod b, we find that the same D_ iso gives a luminosity of 30 with no evolution. Allowing for 2 mag of evolution yields a galaxy with mass corresponding to a 5 galaxy today.

gmw_00011502.name: q0234
gmw_00011502.overview: The high-redshift galaxy q0234 is a quasar host detected at z=4 [arXiv:0909.2630]. It exhibits bright, extended residuals that suggest either a massive exponential host galaxy or an unusually large spheroid with an effective radius much greater than 4 kiloparsecs [arXiv:0909.2630].

sources.summary: The paper treats q0234 as one of four likely z=4 hosts, with bright, extended residuals implying a massive exponential host or an unusually large spheroid.

quotes.quote: We find four likely hosts, and note that they are seen on the images that have the best seeing, FWHM<0 4 , and nearly maximal depth, indicating that we are pushing the limits of detection with these images.

For the bright residuals in q0234, we measure D_ iso 2 6 , while integrating the 1D residuals gives m_K^ Vega (obs) 19.9 -bright enough to represent a 60 exponential (10 with evolution), again with a large scale length.

If we examine the deVaucouleurs curves in Fig. fig-isod we find that the residuals for q0109 and q0234 are too big for their magnitudes observed to be represented by the curves plotted; in other words, if they are spheroids, they must have r_ eff >>4 kpc .

gmw_00011503.name: SDSSpJ084811.52-001418.0
gmw_00011503.overview: SDSSpJ084811.52-001418.0 is a high-redshift quasar with a detected host galaxy [arXiv:0909.2630]. The host galaxy exhibits an H-K color of 0.8, which is bluer than the spectral energy distribution of a redshifted spiral galaxy [arXiv:0909.2630].

sources.summary: The paper reports a detected host for SDSSpJ084811.52-001418.0, finding H-band residual flux consistent with K-band detection and a host color bluer than a redshifted spiral SED.

quotes.quote: Color of the Host Galaxy of SDSSpJ084811.52-001418.0 For one of the quasars with a detected host, SDSSpJ084811.52-001418.0, we also obtained a deep H-band image with PANIC. We carried out the PSF estimation and subtraction from the nuclear quasar image for the H-band, and found a residual flux, consistent with the K-band detection. The color of the host light is H-K=0.8. This color is nominally bluer than that of a redshifted spiral galaxy spectral energy distribution, which would have H-K=2.0.

gmw_00011504.name: q2047
gmw_00011504.overview: The galaxy q2047 is classified as a hyperluminous infrared galaxy [arXiv:0909.2630]. It exhibits asymmetric light residuals extending to the south, which may represent the combined flux of the host galaxy and a companion during a merger [arXiv:0909.2630].

sources.summary: The paper describes q2047 residuals as asymmetric host-plus-companion light, possibly a merger in a hyperluminous infrared galaxy, with luminosity consistent with a massive evolved host.

quotes.quote: For q2047, the residuals extend asymmetrically to the south and possibly represent the combined flux of the host and a companion. This object's classification as a hyperluminous infrared galaxy by (based on sub-mm emission that is likely too strong to originate in the quasar's dust torus) would suggest that we are observing a merger. Integrating the 1D residuals gives m_K^ Vega (obs) 19.7 . The diameter is harder to define because of the asymmetry but we adopt 1 6 as an estimate, which implies a 40 mass, intermediate scale length exponential (7 with evolution).

gmw_00011505.name: SDSSJ1152
gmw_00011505.overview: SDSSJ1152 is a barred spiral galaxy that hosts a distinct bulge component [arXiv:1703.02976]. The galaxy's bulge exhibits low rotation, with a maximum rotational velocity of approximately 50 km/s [arXiv:1703.02976].

sources.summary: The paper identifies SDSSJ1152 as a barred sample galaxy, reports low bulge rotation compared with velocity dispersion, and attributes its Kormendy-relation offset to large parameter errors.

quotes.quote: We detected a bulge component in 7 out of 9 sample galaxies, whereas the remaining 2 (namely SDSSJ1040 and SDSSJ1121) were better fitted only with a disk component. We detected a bar component in 4 out of 9 galaxies (namely SDSSJ1152, SDSSJ1444, SDSSJ1603, and SDSSJ1701).

After correcting for slit orientation and galaxy inclination, we found that the maximum rotational velocity v_ max is much smaller than the corresponding _ e , being v_ max 50 km s ^ 1 for SDSSJ1152 and |v_ max |<30 km s ^ 1 for the remaining galaxies.
The offset of the bulge of SDSSJ1152 is explained by the large errors on r_ e and _ e . Indeed, the data point is consistent within 3 with its magnitude bin.

gmw_00011506.name: HXMM01
gmw_00011506.overview: HXMM01 is a galaxy merger at redshift 2.308 consisting of two massive, gas-rich components connected by a bridge of material [arXiv:1305.4930]. The system exhibits extreme star formation, with an instantaneous star formation rate of approximately 2000 solar masses per year [arXiv:1305.4930].

sources.summary: The paper presents HXMM01 as a weakly lensed z=2.308 merger of two massive, gas-rich SMGs with extreme star formation, high efficiency, and little AGN contribution.

quotes.quote: \ (1HERMES S250 J022016.5 060143) was identified as an unusually bright SMG in the \ Multi-tiered Extragalactic Survey (HerMES ). We observed it with a variety of ground-based telescopes from optical to radio to obtain higher resolution images and to better sample the spectral energy distribution (SED). The source is resolved into two similarly bright components at z 2.308 separated by 3 \ (Figure fig:obs ; hereafter \ and \ for the northern and southern component, respectively), which are connected by a bridge of material reminiscent of tidal tails frequently seen in galaxy mergers. The Carbon Monoxide (CO) J = 1 0, J = 4 3, and H spectra all show slightly different redshifts for these two components. The velocity separation is 260 70 \ and 330 220 \ from the \ and H spectra, respectively. In addition, the \ line widths for the two components are significantly different ( 970 150 \ vs. 660 100 ), establishing clearly that they are two distinct galaxies undergoing a merger.
This luminosity implies an instantaneous star formation rate (SFR) of 2000 400 \ for a Chabrier initial mass function.

gmw_00011507.name: G2
gmw_00011507.overview: G2 is an elliptical galaxy classified as a Seyfert 2 active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1901.04683]. It is part of a foreground pair of galaxies, and a tidal tail connects G2 to a background quasar [arXiv:1901.04683].

sources.summary: The paper identifies G2 as the Seyfert component of G0248, connected by a tidal tail to the background quasar, lacking systematic rotation, and spectrally elliptical.

quotes.quote: The absorption comes from a foreground pair of galaxies called G0248 Junkkarinen1987 , consisting of the two sources G1 and G2. A tidal tail connects, in projection, G2 with the background quasar.

Indeed, G1 is a LINER and G2 a Seyfert 2, and based on their optical emission line excitation both are located in the AGN region of the Baldwin-Phillips-Terlevich (BPT) diagram Baldwin1981 , plotting [ OIII ] /H as a function of [ NII ] /H .

determine the kinematics of the two merging galaxies, the LINER G1 and Seyfert G2; the former shows systematic rotation, but the latter does not. With respect to G2 (z=0.0507), a constant velocity of 100 \ is observed all across the tidal tail. Furthermore, from their spectra, they conclude that G1 is a spiral galaxy and G2 an elliptical.

gmw_00011508.name: RA/Dec = 20:39:09.12/ 30:45:20.84 (J2000)
gmw_00011508.overview: RA/Dec = 20:39:09.12/ 30:45:20.84 (J2000) is a galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.0484 [arXiv:1602.01088]. Mid-infrared colors and X-ray observations indicate that the galaxy does not host a strong active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1602.01088].

sources.summary: The coordinate-identified host galaxy of ASASSN-15oi is placed at z=0.0484, lacks evidence for strong AGN activity, and has a fitted stellar mass of about 1.1e10 solar masses.

quotes.quote: The object's position corresponds to , a galaxy with no previous redshift measurement.

Although the redshift was originally reported as z=0.02 , our later spectra showed that the redshift of the host galaxy is z=0.0484 , corresponding to a luminosity distance of 216 Mpc ( H_0=69.6 km s ^ 1 Mpc ^ 1 , _M=0.29 , _ =0.71 ).

Examining data from the ROSAT All-Sky Survey voges99 , we do not detect the host galaxy to a 3-sigma upper limit of 2.0 10^ 2 counts s ^ 1 in the 0.3-10.0 keV band. This corresponds to a limit of 1.2 10^ 13 ergs s ^ 1 cm ^ 2 ( L_X<6.7 10^ 41 ergs s ^ 1 ), which provides evidence that the galaxy does not host a strong AGN. Detections of the host in Wide-field Infrared Survey Explorer WISE; wright10 data corroborate this picture, as the galaxy has a mid-IR (MIR) color of (W1-W2) 0.06 0.06 , implying that any AGN activity in the galaxy is not strong e.g., assef13 . Using the code for Fitting and Assessment of Synthetic Templates FAST v1.0; kriek09 , we fit stellar population synthesis (SPS) models to the archival GALEX NUV limit, ASAS-SN V band measurement, and 2MASS JHK_s magnitudes of the host galaxy. The fit was done assuming a cardelli88 extinction law with R_V=3.1 and a Galactic extinction of A_V=0.19 mag based on schlafly11 , an exponentially declining star-formation history, a Salpeter IMF, and the bruzual03 models.

gmw_00011509.name: UDG 432
gmw_00011509.overview: UDG 432 is an ultra-diffuse galaxy located in the Coma cluster [arXiv:2102.11285]. An X-ray source is located within 5 arcseconds of its center, though it lacks an optical counterpart and may be a foreground or background object [arXiv:2102.11285].

sources.summary: The paper lists UDG 432 among Coma UDGs with a nearby X-ray source, but warns such matches may be foreground or background and reports no optical counterpart.

quotes.quote: We detect three X-ray sources with detection significance 5 that could be off-centre AGN within 5 arcsec from the centre of the UDG 317, UDG 432, and UDG 535.

It is possible that the X-ray sources associated with the UDG 317, UDG 432, and UDG 535 are not AGN in these galaxies, but are foreground or background objects. Therefore, their existence within a radius of 5 arcsec from the centre of the UDGs is due to random matches.

However, no optical counterparts were detected for the X-ray sources associated with the UDG 432 and UDG 535. Also, we checked images of these UDGs in FIR and other wave bands, but we did not find any significant sources in the field of these galaxies.

gmw_00011510.name: UDG 535
gmw_00011510.overview: UDG 535 is a ultra-diffuse dwarf galaxy located in the Coma cluster [arXiv:2102.11285]. An X-ray source is detected within 5 arcseconds of its center, though it lacks optical or far-infrared counterparts and may be a random foreground or background alignment [arXiv:2102.11285].

sources.summary: The paper lists UDG 535 among Coma UDGs with a nearby X-ray source, but treats the association as uncertain and finds no optical or other counterpart.

quotes.quote: We detect three X-ray sources with detection significance 5 that could be off-centre AGN within 5 arcsec from the centre of the UDG 317, UDG 432, and UDG 535.

It is possible that the X-ray sources associated with the UDG 317, UDG 432, and UDG 535 are not AGN in these galaxies, but are foreground or background objects. Therefore, their existence within a radius of 5 arcsec from the centre of the UDGs is due to random matches.

However, no optical counterparts were detected for the X-ray sources associated with the UDG 432 and UDG 535. Also, we checked images of these UDGs in FIR and other wave bands, but we did not find any significant sources in the field of these galaxies.

gmw_00011511.overview: The BL Lacertae object 0652+426 features a two-sided radio jet embedded in a bright halo [arXiv:0711.1158]. High-resolution imaging reveals a well-collimated inner jet with detected polarization, though its magnetic field direction remains unclear [arXiv:0711.1158].

sources.summary: The paper reports that 0652+426 has a two-sided halo-embedded VLA jet, a well-collimated VLBI jet, and inner-jet polarization with unclear magnetic-field alignment.

quotes.quote: 0652+426 The 1.4 GHz VLA image of this source shows a two-sided jet source embedded in a bright halo, with the brighter jet at PA=40 Rector03 . Our VLBI image (Fig.  fig:0652 ) shows a well-collimated jet extending from the core in PA 40 . Polarization is detected in the inner jet, with the inferred B field showing no obvious relation to the jet direction.

gmw_00011512.overview: The source 0749+540 is classified as a BL Lacertae object based on its featureless optical spectrum and high optical polarization [arXiv:0711.1158]. It has a lower redshift limit of z > 0.2 and features a dominant VLBI core with a northern jet extension [arXiv:0711.1158].

sources.summary: The paper identifies 0749+540 as a BL Lac with high optical polarization, a lower redshift limit, and a VLBI core plus northern extension with highly polarized core emission.

quotes.quote: 0749+540 This source was classified as a BL Lac object by KuehrSchmidt90 based on its featureless optical spectrum and high optical polarization. A lower limit of z>0.2 for its redshift was derived by StickelKuhr93 based on the absence of any extended structure of a host galaxy in a direct image taken with the 3.5m Calar Alto telescope. The 5 GHz VLBI image of Taylor94 shows a jet emerging from the core in PA 0 . Our VLBI image (Fig.  fig:0749 ) reveals a dominant core, with an extension to the north, in roughly the direction of the previously detected VLBI jet. The core shows an unusually high fractional polarization of 9 , while no polarization was detected in the jet.

gmw_00011513.name: J0109--5424
gmw_00011513.overview: J0109--5424 is a broad-absorption line quasar candidate at an estimated redshift of z=6.07 [arXiv:2403.17903]. It exhibits flux below the Lyman limit and in the g-band, which is interpreted as evidence of a contaminating foreground lensing galaxy [arXiv:2403.17903].

sources.summary: The paper reports J0109--5424 as a z=6.07 BAL quasar candidate likely lensed by foreground galaxy contamination, supported by below-Lyman-limit flux, SED modelling, and short near-zone size.

quotes.quote: Notably, J0109--5424 shows an excess of flux in the Lyman- forest and below the Lyman continuum limit, consistent with the g and band detection which we interpret as evidence for a foreground lensing galaxy (see further Section  sec:lensed_quasar ).

We fit J0603--3923 with the `median' quasar template of Banados2016 and J0109--5424 with the `weak' template, deriving best-fitting redshifts of z=5.94 and 6.07 , respectively. The error of these templates compared to the Mg ii or [ C ii ] redshifts is typically z<0.03 banados23 . Both quasars are detected in the band, and J0109--5424 also in the g band, which may have led them to be discarded as contaminants in traditional colour-cut searches.

Most interestingly, the spectrum of J0109--5424 presents significant flux throughout the Lyman- forest and even below the Lyman limit. This is an unambiguous signature of a foreground, potentially lensing, galaxy contaminating the photometry of the quasar, making J0109--5424 the first lensed BAL quasar candidate at z>5.5 . Further, the SExtractor class star probability (0: extended source, 1: point-source) for the g band is 0.32 , suggesting that the contaminating object is likely a galaxy For the rizY bands, the class star probabilities are respectively 0.82 , 0.96 , 0.98 , 0.84 , consistent with the point-source appearance of the quasar in these bands. . SED modelling also suggests J0109--5424 to be lensed, unlike J0603--3923 or J0122--4609.

gmw_00011514.name: J0122--4609
gmw_00011514.overview: J0122--4609 is a newly discovered high-redshift quasar [arXiv:2403.17903]. Spectroscopic observations reveal a clear Lyman break and a blue rest-frame ultraviolet color, indicating a redshift of z=5.99 [arXiv:2403.17903].

sources.summary: The paper confirms J0122--4609 as a newly discovered high-redshift quasar using archival EFOSC2/NTT spectroscopy, identifying a Lyman break and fitting z=5.99.

quotes.quote: We found that J0122--4609 had unpublished EFOSC2/NTT spectroscopy (programme 098.A-0439(A), PI: McMahon). It was observed on 2016 December 22/23 for 1200 s using the 1.0 arcsec wide slit with grism 16 and the OG530 blocking filter.

The spectrum clearly shows the Lyman break and blue rest-frame UV colour of a high-redshift quasar (see Fig.  fig:all_spectra , second panel). We fit the spectrum of J0122--4609 with the `median' quasar template of Banados2016 , deriving a redshift of z=5.99 .

gmw_00011515.name: J0603--3923
gmw_00011515.overview: J0603--3923 is a high-redshift quasar located at a redshift of approximately z=5.94 [arXiv:2403.17903]. Optical spectroscopy of the source reveals a clear Lyman break that is typical of quasars at this redshift [arXiv:2403.17903].

sources.summary: The paper confirms J0603--3923 as a z=5.94 high-redshift quasar from GMOS spectroscopy showing a clear Lyman break and successful quasar-template fitting.

quotes.quote: Two further candidates on `Quasar Island', J0603--3923 and J0109--5424, were observed on 2022 December 6 and 11 respectively with the Gemini Multi-Object Spectrograph (GMOS) South (Programme ID GS-2022B-FT-208, PI: Farina). The two candidates were observed in long-slit mode for 4 300 s exposures using the 1.5 arcsec slit width. We used the R400+G5325 grating in combination with the GG455 G0329 blocking filter to cover the wavelength range 5300 10300 where the Lyman break of a z 6 quasar would be expected.
We show the optical GMOS spectra of these two sources in Fig.  fig:all_spectra , revealing a clear Lyman- break typical of z 6 quasars.

We fit J0603--3923 with the `median' quasar template of Banados2016 and J0109--5424 with the `weak' template, deriving best-fitting redshifts of z=5.94 and 6.07 , respectively. The error of these templates compared to the Mg ii or [ C ii ] redshifts is typically z<0.03 banados23 .

gmw_00011516.name: HSC-XD 52
gmw_00011516.overview: HSC-XD 52 is a spectroscopically confirmed low-mass active galactic nucleus at an intermediate redshift of z ~ 0.56 [arXiv:1910.02173]. The host galaxy is in the dwarf regime with a stellar mass of approximately 3 billion solar masses [arXiv:1910.02173].

sources.summary: The paper presents HSC-XD 52 as a spectroscopically confirmed low-mass AGN at z 0.56, with dwarf-like stellar mass, high X-ray luminosity, and declining activity over time.

quotes.quote: In this letter, we present HSC-XD 52, a spectroscopically confirmed low-mass active galactic nucleus (AGN) at an intermediate redshift of z 0.56 . We detect this object as a very luminous X-ray source coincident with a galaxy observed by the Hyper Suprime-Cam (HSC) as part of a broader search for low-mass AGN. We constrain its stellar mass through spectral energy distribution modeling to be LMC-like at M_ 3 10^9 M_ , placing it in the dwarf regime.

We estimate a central black hole mass of M_ BH 10^ 6 M_ . With an average X-ray luminosity of L_X 3.5 10^ 43 erg s ^ 1 , HSC-XD 52 is among the most luminous X-ray selected AGN in dwarf galaxies. The spectroscopic and photometric properties of HSC-XD 52 indicate that it is an intermediate redshift counterpart to local low-mass AGN.

gmw_00011517.name: NGC 428
gmw_00011517.overview: NGC 428 is a late-type barred spiral galaxy with a highly perturbed morphology [arXiv:1810.12944]. Although its ionized gas rotates circularly, the galaxy exhibits a circumnuclear inclined disc and signs of a recent merger or accretion event [arXiv:1810.12944, arXiv:astro-ph/0604138].

sources.summary: The paper studies NGC 428 as a perturbed late-type barred spiral whose ionized gas mostly rotates circularly, but with local feedback motions and signs of recent accretion.

quotes.quote: To demonstrate our approach, we considered the brightest sample galaxy NGC 428, a late-type barred spiral with several morphological peculiarities. We analysed the radial metallicity distribution, the ionized gas line-of-sight velocity and velocity dispersion maps together with WISE and SDSS images. Despite its very perturbed morphology, the velocity field of ionized gas in NGC 428 is well described by pure circular rotation in a thin flat disc with streaming motions in the central bar.

We also found some local non-circular gas motions clearly related to stellar feedback processes. At the same time, we revealed a circumnuclear inclined disc in NGC 428 and a region with significant residual velocities that could be considered as a result of a recent ( <0.5 Gyr) accretion event. The observed oxygen abundance gradient does not contradict this conclusion.

sources.summary: The paper finds NGC 428 unusual in stellar-population index space, describes its old nuclear cluster, high extinction, recent-merger interpretation, and minimal young stellar mass.

quotes.quote: The one object that falls out of the region covered by SSPs and continuous star formation histories alike in Figure f:metage is NGC 428 at a D _n (4000) value of 1.85. This could possibly arise from the extinction effect described in MacArthur (2005), as we indeed find an unusually high extinction for this NC in Section s:mixpop .

While the NC in NGC 428 is rather old and we would therefore expect it not to be surrounded by a lot of gas, the galaxy NGC 428 as a whole probably is a recent merger and is forming stars at a rather high rate (Smoker et al. 1996), implying a gas and dust reservoir to be present in this galaxy. The spectral indices shown in Figure f:metage imply a significant amount of extinction for this object.
The division into 3 old and 6 young nuclei is however still valid in the sense that NGC 300, NGC 428 and NGC 3423 all have less than 3 of their mass in any population younger than 1 Gyr.

gmw_00011518.name: NGC 1493
gmw_00011518.overview: NGC 1493 is a spiral galaxy characterized by a young galactic nucleus [arXiv:astro-ph/0604138]. The stellar population of this nucleus has an estimated luminosity-weighted mean age of approximately 0.1 Gyr, with a recent-burst mass of 10^4.0 solar masses [arXiv:astro-ph/0604138].

sources.summary: The paper places NGC 1493 among young-nucleus systems, estimates its recent-burst mass, and notes that VLT and STIS fits give substantially different luminosity-weighted ages.

quotes.quote: Thus, both suggest a distinction into two groups of nuclei: those that are 0.1 Gyr (NGC 1042, NGC 1493, NGC 2139, NGC 7418, NGC 7424, NGC 7793) and those that are 1 Gyr (NGC 300, NGC 428, NGC 3423).

In order of ascending NGC number, we find the following log(M/M _ ): NGC 300:4.5 - NGC 428:4.4 - NGC 1042:3.9 - NGC 1493:4.0 - NGC 2139:4.8 - NGC 3423:3.7 - NGC 7418:6.2 - NGC 7424:5.1 - NGC 7793:6.1 (mean log(M/M _ ) = 5.5).

We do note that for NGC 1493 and NGC 2139 the VLT and STIS spectra give substantially different results for _ 10 . This is not because the spectra or the fits are very different. Instead, the reason is that for spectra that are dominated by young light, the luminosity-weighted mean age is very sensitive to even a small fraction of the light in a very old population. Small differences between the light fractions in the oldest populations (which are poorly constrained at the 10 level) for the VLT and STIS fits generate large differences in _ 10 ( 1.97 dex for NGC 1493 and 0.82 dex for NGC 2139).

gmw_00011519.name: NGC 2139
gmw_00011519.overview: NGC 2139 is a spiral galaxy containing a nuclear cluster that is possibly in the process of forming [arXiv:astro-ph/0604138]. The light of this cluster is dominated by a very young stellar population with a super-solar metallicity [arXiv:astro-ph/0604138].

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 2139 as a young, possibly forming nuclear cluster with little old light, low mass-to-light ratio, the youngest SSP age, and super-solar metallicity.

quotes.quote: The most notable result is that in NGC 2139 even a modest contribution of 10 of the blue light from any population 0.1 Gyr is ruled out, in qualitative agreement with the low M/L _I derived dynamically. It is quite possible therefore that NGC 2139 contains a NC that is in the process of forming. However, it should be kept in mind that the light of this cluster is dominated by a very bright and young population with a low M/L . The best-fit SSP age is the youngest in our sample ( = 6.78 ).

It is also striking that the NC in NGC 2139 is the only one, where we measure a super-solar metallicity. Taking this result at face value and admitting the cluster was forming right now, would require an unusually high metallicity for the gas that falls onto the cluster to form the stars.

gmw_00011520.name: NGC 3423
gmw_00011520.overview: NGC 3423 is a galaxy characterized by an older nuclear cluster [arXiv:astro-ph/0604138]. Its nucleus has less than three percent of its mass in stellar populations younger than 1 Gyr [arXiv:astro-ph/0604138].

sources.summary: The paper groups NGC 3423 with older nuclear clusters, states that it has little mass in populations younger than 1 Gyr, and estimates its last-burst mass.

quotes.quote: Thus, both suggest a distinction into two groups of nuclei: those that are 0.1 Gyr (NGC 1042, NGC 1493, NGC 2139, NGC 7418, NGC 7424, NGC 7793) and those that are 1 Gyr (NGC 300, NGC 428, NGC 3423).
The division into 3 old and 6 young nuclei is however still valid in the sense that NGC 300, NGC 428 and NGC 3423 all have less than 3 of their mass in any population younger than 1 Gyr.

In order of ascending NGC number, we find the following log(M/M _ ): NGC 300:4.5 - NGC 428:4.4 - NGC 1042:3.9 - NGC 1493:4.0 - NGC 2139:4.8 - NGC 3423:3.7 - NGC 7418:6.2 - NGC 7424:5.1 - NGC 7793:6.1 (mean log(M/M _ ) = 5.5).

gmw_00011521.name: SDSS J215314.00-064338.3
gmw_00011521.overview: SDSS J215314.00-064338.3 is a galaxy with a photometric redshift estimated at 0.208 by SDSS [arXiv:2012.11700]. PanSTARRS observations report a corresponding photometric redshift of 0.168, and the galaxy is coincident with the transient AT2019aaig [arXiv:2012.11700].

sources.summary: The paper identifies SDSS J215314.00-064338.3 as coincident with AT2019aaig, gives SDSS and PanSTARRS photometric redshifts, and reports a 0.5 arcsecond offset.

quotes.quote: Searching the literature, we find AT2019aaig is coincident with the galaxy SDSS J215314.00-064338.3 SDSSDR12 . SDSS J215314.00-064338.3 has a z_ photo = 0.208 0.030, within 3 of the inferred LIGO/Virgo distance for this event. Searching PanSTARRS DR2, we find a z_ photo = 0.168 0.041, in agreement with the SDSS z_ photo , and in better agreement with the inferred LIGO/Virgo distance. We find an 0.5'' between the position of AT2019aaig and the position of the galaxy from SDSS DR12.

gmw_00011522.name: IRAS 04296+2923
gmw_00011522.overview: IRAS 04296+2923 is a nearby barred spiral galaxy and a luminous infrared galaxy located behind the Taurus molecular cloud [arXiv:1008.5174]. It is highly gas-rich with strong CO emission, hosting a nuclear starburst concentrated within 150 to 250 parsecs of its dynamical center [arXiv:1008.5174].

sources.summary: The paper presents IRAS 04296+2923 as a nearby barred LIRG with a bar-driven nuclear starburst, strong CO emission, abundant molecular gas, and star formation concentrated near the dynamical center.

quotes.quote: We present a first look at the local luminous infrared galaxy IRAS 04296+2923. This barred spiral galaxy, overlooked because of its location behind the Taurus molecular cloud, is among the half dozen closest (D= 29 Mpc) LIRGs. More IR-luminous than either M82 or the Antennae, it may be the best local example of a nuclear starburst caused by bar-mediated secular evolution.

IRAS 04296+2923 is very bright in CO, and among the most gas-rich galaxies in the local universe. The ^ 12 CO luminosity of the inner half kpc is equivalent to that of the entire Milky Way. While the most intense CO emission is extended over a 15 \ (2 kpc) diameter region, the nuclear starburst is confined to within 1--2 \ (150--250 pc) of the dynamical center.

gmw_00011523.name: SDSS J075654.53+341543.6
gmw_00011523.overview: SDSS J075654.53+341543.6 is a host galaxy with an estimated stellar mass of 1.4 billion solar masses [arXiv:1808.02890]. The galaxy has a stellar population age of approximately 5.0 billion years and hosts the nuclear tidal disruption event candidate PS18kh [arXiv:1808.02890].

sources.summary: The paper identifies SDSS J075654.53+341543.6 as PS18kh's host, places the transient near its nucleus, and models the host SED to estimate stellar mass, age, and star formation rate.

quotes.quote: Here we present the discovery of PS18kh, a TDE candidate discovered by the Pan-STARRS Survey for Transients https://star.pst.qub.ac.uk/ps1threepi/psdb/ PSST; chambers16 on 2018 March 02 in the spectroscopically unobserved galaxy SDSS J075654.53+341543.6. The discovery was announced publicly on 2018 March 04 on the Transient Name Server (TNS) and given the designation AT 2018zr https://wis-tns.weizmann.ac.il/object/2018zr . The discovery image indicated that the position of the transient was consistent with the nucleus of the host, with the Pan-STARRS coordinates lying within 0 1 of the measured center of the host in SDSS.

We fit the spectral energy distribution (SED) of the host galaxy to the archival limits and magnitudes from GALEX, SDSS, and WISE using the publicly available Fitting and Assessment of Synthetic Templates fast ; kriek09 . For the fit we assumed a cardelli89 extinction law with R_V=3.1 and a Galactic extinction of A_V = 0.128 mag schlafly11 and we adopted an exponentially declining star-formation history, a Salpeter initial mass function, and the bruzual03 stellar population models. In order to make a more robust estimate of the host SED and the uncertainties on its physical parameters, we generated 1000 realizations of the archival fluxes, perturbed by their respective uncertainties assuming Gaussian errors. Each realization was then modeled with fast . The median and 68 confidence intervals on the host parameters from these 1000 realizations are: M_ =1.4^ +0.4 _ 0.4 10^ 10 M _ , age =5.0^ +2.1 _ 1.9 Gyr, and a star formation rate SFR = 6.8^ +4.0 _ 4.9 10^ 3 M _ yr ^ 1 .

gmw_00011524.name: UN J1025 0040
gmw_00011524.overview: UN J1025 0040 is a post-starburst quasar with a host galaxy that resembles a merger remnant [arXiv:astro-ph/0603218]. It has an interacting companion galaxy also in a post-starburst phase, which may have triggered both the quasar activity and its nuclear starburst [arXiv:astro-ph/0603218].

sources.summary: The review presents UN J1025 0040 as a post-starburst quasar with a 400 Myr luminous starburst, an interacting post-starburst companion, and an HST host resembling a merger remnant.

quotes.quote: A striking case is UN J1025 0040 bro99 . The strong starburst component has an age of 400 Myr, and a bolometric luminosity of 10^ 11.6 L _ , equal to that of the quasar. A younger UN J1025 0040 (tens of Myr after the starburst) would have a more luminous stellar population and would likely be dust enshrouded, placing it in the ULIRG class.

UN J1025 0040 has a nearby companion galaxy also in a post-starburst phase can00 . The companion appears to be interacting with the quasar host galaxy, and this interaction may have triggered both the starburst and the quasar activity in UN J1025 0040. HST WFPC2 imaging bro02 shows that the starburst is nuclear, the host resembles a merger remnant, and a less massive young starburst is also present.

gmw_00011525.overview: The object 1842+681 is an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1509.02621]. Very-long-baseline interferometry observations reveal a core-dominated radio structure with some elongation aligned in the direction of its jet [arXiv:1509.02621].

sources.summary: The paper treats 1842+681 as one of five AGN targets observed by KVN at 22-130 GHz for multi-frequency VLBI astrometry, map registration, position shifts, and jet-related alignment tests.

quotes.quote: Figure fig:source_pos shows the distribution in the sky of the five selected sources (1803+784, 1807+698, 1842+681, 1928+738 and 2007+777) along with their angular separations, which range between 3.6^o and 11^o . None of the sources had been observed previously with VLBI at 130 GHz. The observations consisted of 3 minutes long scans alternating between the sources in each of the two triangles shown in Figure fig:source_pos , and between the triangles ca. every hour and a half, at the four bands simultaneously. Alternating between multiple sources allowed us to develop a strategy to decompose the relative astrometric measurements into single source position shifts that allow, for example, the registration of the images at different frequencies.

Figure fig:sc shows self-calibration images for the five AGN sources in the multi-frequency KVN observations, including the first images of these sources at 130 GHz. The visibility datasets were modelfitted and imaged and show little divergence from point-source core dominated images; in some cases there appears to be some elongation aligned with the jet direction as seen in higher resolution maps from MOJAVE mojave . Table tab:flux lists the total flux values as measured from the maps, at all frequency bands.

gmw_00011526.name: A1703-zd6
gmw_00011526.overview: A1703-zd6 is a low-mass, gravitationally lensed galaxy located at a redshift of z = 7.045 [arXiv:2301.03599]. It exhibits a small red-peak separation of 60 km/s, which is consistent with a high Lyman-continuum escape fraction [arXiv:2301.03599].

sources.summary: The paper describes A1703-zd6 as a z=7.045 lensed, low-mass galaxy excluded from the stack but having a small red-peak separation consistent with high LyC escape.

quotes.quote: Additionally, Schenker+12 reports the detection of A1703-zd6, a redshift z = 7.045 galaxy that satisfies all of our selection criteria, except one - it is a lensed, low-mass galaxy ( Stark+15 ( M_ ) 8.7 ). We therefore do not include this target within our stack, but we note the measured red-peak separation from line-center of 60 km/s. This is consistent with a high Lyman-continuum escape fraction as expected if faint galaxies are responsible for re-ionization Gazagnes+20, Izotov+18a .

gmw_00011527.name: Object 38
gmw_00011527.overview: Object 38 is classified as a galaxy merger [arXiv:2002.00967]. Its position-velocity diagram reveals multiple components separated by 250 km/s, and its optical light is offset by 0.5 arcseconds from the peak of its [CII] emission [arXiv:2002.00967].

sources.summary: The appendix describes Object 38 as a merger, noting [CII] emission, UV/optical alignment, multiple components in the ALPINE imaging and spectra.

quotes.quote: Object 38 : this object is classified as a merger because multiple components, separated by 250 km s ^ 1 , are present in the position-velocity diagram (see Le F evre et al. 2020). The optical light is offset by 0.5" from the peak of the [ CII ] emission.

gmw_00011528.name: Object 5100537582
gmw_00011528.overview: Object 5100537582 is a galaxy classified as dispersion dominated [arXiv:2002.00967]. Its optical emission is coincident with the peak of its [CII] emission map, and it shows no evidence of multiple components [arXiv:2002.00967].

sources.summary: The appendix describes Object 5100537582 as dispersion dominated, noting [CII] emission, UV/optical alignment, multiple components in the ALPINE imaging and spectra.

quotes.quote: Object 5100537582 : this object is classified as dispersion dominated. The optical emission is coincident with the peak of the [ CII ] map. There is no evidence of multiple components.

gmw_00011529.name: Object 5100994794
gmw_00011529.overview: Object 5100994794 is a dispersion-dominated galaxy [arXiv:2002.00967]. It exhibits extended [CII] emission that is well centered within the spectroscopic slit [arXiv:2002.00967].

sources.summary: The appendix describes Object 5100994794 as dispersion dominated, noting [CII] emission in the ALPINE imaging and spectra.

quotes.quote: Object 5100994794 : this object is classified as dispersion dominated. The [ CII ] emission is quite extended and well centered in the spectroscopic slit. The and [ CII ] spectra are extracted from the same physical region.

gmw_00011530.name: Object 510786441
gmw_00011530.overview: Object 510786441 is classified as a galaxy merger [arXiv:2002.00967]. This system features multiple components detected in its position-velocity diagram, with its optical emission spatially aligned with the peak of its [CII] emission [arXiv:2002.00967].

sources.summary: The appendix describes Object 510786441 as a merger, noting [CII] emission, UV/optical alignment, multiple components in the ALPINE imaging and spectra.

quotes.quote: Object 510786441 : this object is classified as a merger because multiple components are detected in the position-velocity diagram (Le F evre et al. 2020), separated by 200 km s ^ 1 . The optical emission is spatially coincident with the peak of the [ CII ] emission.

gmw_00011531.name: Object 351640
gmw_00011531.overview: Object 351640 is classified as a dispersion-dominated galaxy [arXiv:2002.00967]. It is very weak in both optical and near-infrared imaging, and it shows no evidence of multiple components [arXiv:2002.00967].

sources.summary: The appendix describes Object 351640 as dispersion dominated, noting [CII] emission, UV/optical alignment, multiple components in the ALPINE imaging and spectra.

quotes.quote: Object 351640 : this object is classified as a dispersion-dominated galaxy. It is very weak both in the optical and in the NIR image, which is expected because it is selected as emitter by the narrow-band technique, and therefore it is difficult to identify possible spatial offsets between and [ CII ] . There is no evidence of multiple components.

gmw_00011532.name: Object 403030
gmw_00011532.overview: Object 403030 is classified as a galaxy merger [arXiv:2002.00967]. Optical imaging tracing the rest-frame ultraviolet emission reveals multiple components, while its spectral profile exhibits double peaks arising from the same spatial region [arXiv:2002.00967].

sources.summary: The appendix describes Object 403030 as a merger, noting UV/optical alignment, multiple components in the ALPINE imaging and spectra.

quotes.quote: Object 403030 : this object is classified as a merger mainly because the optical SUBARU images, tracing the UV rest-frame, show multiple components (green contours in the figure). The spectral profile is double peaked (the main peak lies at +400 km s ^ 1 , the other at 200 km s ^ 1 ), but we confirmed in the 2D spectrum that there is no spatial offsets between the two peaks, meaning that within the resolution, they arise from the same region. Double-peaked profiles are easily predicted in the framework of the homogeneous expanding shell model (Verhamme et al. 2008).

gmw_00011533.name: Object 416105
gmw_00011533.overview: Object 416105 is classified as a rotator [arXiv:2002.00967]. The galaxy exhibits [CII] emission with only small, insignificant spatial offsets between its UV and optical rest-frame emission [arXiv:2002.00967].

sources.summary: The appendix describes Object 416105 as a rotator, noting [CII] emission, UV/optical alignment, multiple components in the ALPINE imaging and spectra.

quotes.quote: Object 416105 : this object is classified as a rotator. Small and insignificant spatial offsets are present between the UV and optical rest-frame and the [ CII ] emission. There is no evidence of multiple components.

gmw_00011534.name: Object 422677
gmw_00011534.overview: Object 422677 is classified as a galaxy merger [arXiv:2002.00967]. Its position-velocity diagram reveals multiple kinematic components separated in velocity by less than 200 km/s, while its [CII] emission is coincident with the optical rest-frame light [arXiv:2002.00967].

sources.summary: The appendix describes Object 422677 as a merger, noting [CII] emission, UV/optical alignment, multiple components in the ALPINE imaging and spectra.

quotes.quote: Object 422677 : this object is classified as a merger because multiple kinematic components are present in the position velocity diagram, separated in velocity by less than 200 km s ^ 1 (see Le F evre et al. 2020). However, the [ CII ] emission is coincident with the optical rest-frame light and is only slightly offset from the UV rest-frame emission.

gmw_00011535.name: Object 430951
gmw_00011535.overview: Object 430951 is a faint galaxy that is too faint in its [CII] map to be morpho-kinematically classified [arXiv:2002.00967]. Its [CII] emission is offset from the spectroscopic slit position and features a wide spectrum with a red tail [arXiv:2002.00967].

sources.summary: The appendix describes Object 430951 as too faint for morpho-kinematic classification, noting [CII] emission in the ALPINE imaging and spectra.

quotes.quote: Object 430951 : this object is too faint in the [ CII ] map to be morpho-kinematically classified. The [ CII ] spectrum is quite wide, with a red tail extending up to +1500 km s ^ 1 from the spectral peak. Moreover, the [ CII ] emission is offset from the position of the spectroscopic slit, therefore [ CII ] and in this case do not originate from the same physical region. Interestingly, this is one of the few cases in which we measure a negative [ CII ] velocity offset.

gmw_00011536.name: Object 488399
gmw_00011536.overview: Object 488399 is a dispersion-dominated galaxy characterized by a very weak ultraviolet continuum [arXiv:2002.00967]. Its ionized carbon emission is significantly more extended than its optical light, and it is classified as a narrow-band emitter [arXiv:2002.00967].

sources.summary: The appendix describes Object 488399 as dispersion dominated, noting [CII] emission, UV/optical alignment in the ALPINE imaging and spectra.

quotes.quote: Object 488399 : this object is classified as dispersion dominated, and the [ CII ] emission is much more extended than the optical light. This object is selected as a narrow-band emitter, with a very weak UV continuum, and therefore it is not detected in the optical images. For this object, we re-extracted the [ CII ] spectrum from a smaller region, coincident with the K_s emission, but the position of the [ CII ] velocity peak did not change.

gmw_00011537.name: Object 493583
gmw_00011537.overview: Object 493583 is classified as a galaxy merger [arXiv:2002.00967]. Its position-velocity diagram reveals two distinct dynamical components separated in velocity by 200 km/s, while its UV-optical rest-frame and [CII] emissions are spatially coincident [arXiv:2002.00967].

sources.summary: The appendix describes Object 493583 as a merger, noting [CII] emission, UV/optical alignment, multiple components in the ALPINE imaging and spectra.

quotes.quote: Object 493583 : this object is classified as a merger mainly because two dynamical components are present in the position-velocity diagram, separated in velocity by 200 km s ^ 1 (see Le F evre et al. 2020). The UV-optical rest-frame and [ CII ] emissions are all spatially coincident.

gmw_00011538.name: Object 494763
gmw_00011538.overview: Object 494763 is classified as a dispersion-dominated system [arXiv:2002.00967]. Its ultraviolet-optical rest-frame and [CII] emissions are spatially coincident, and the galaxy exhibits a double-peaked spectral profile [arXiv:2002.00967].

sources.summary: The appendix describes Object 494763 as dispersion dominated, noting [CII] emission, UV/optical alignment in the ALPINE imaging and spectra.

quotes.quote: Object 494763 : this object is classified as dispersion dominated. The UV-optical rest-frame and [ CII ] emissions are all spatially coincident. The spectral profile is double peaked, as it often is in models of homogeneous expanding shells (Verhamme et al. 2008).

gmw_00011539.name: Object 510660
gmw_00011539.overview: Object 510660 is a galaxy characterized by very faint [CII] emission [arXiv:2002.00967]. Its optical rest-frame emission is coincident with the peak of this [CII] emission, and the galaxy shows no signs of multiple components in its images or spectra [arXiv:2002.00967].

sources.summary: The appendix describes Object 510660 with resolved emission properties, noting [CII] emission, UV/optical alignment, multiple components in the ALPINE imaging and spectra.

quotes.quote: Object 510660 : the [ CII ] emission for this object is very faint, and therefore it was not possible to obtain a morpho-kinematic classification. The [ CII ] spectrum is also quite noisy. The optical rest-frame emission is coincident with the peak of the [ CII ] emission. There are no signs of multiple components from the images or the spectra.

gmw_00011540.name: Object 539609
gmw_00011540.overview: Object 539609 is classified as a rotator [arXiv:2002.00967]. Its optical emission is well aligned with its [CII] emission, showing no evidence of multiple components and only a very small spectral [CII] offset [arXiv:2002.00967].

sources.summary: The appendix describes Object 539609 as a rotator, noting [CII] emission, UV/optical alignment, multiple components in the ALPINE imaging and spectra.

quotes.quote: Object 539609 : this object is classified as a rotator. The optical emission is well aligned with the [ CII ] emission, and there is no evidence of multiple components. The spectral [ CII ] offset is also very small.
